1996.assessment:
Post-1994 civil war debt elevated. Budget running deficits from security
spending and oil revenue uncertainty. High debt levels but oil revenues
providing some stability. Moderate deficit with elevated debt.

1997.assessment:
Fiscal consolidation efforts. Oil revenues improving. Debt/GDP declining
with higher nominal GDP. IMF/World Bank programs helping fiscal stability.
Elevated debt but improving.

1998.assessment:
Oil price crash causes severe fiscal crisis. Revenue collapses. Budget
deficit surges. Government forced to cut subsidies triggering protests.
Debt/GDP ratio jumps. Large deficit, high debt.

1999.assessment:
Some fiscal recovery as oil prices rise. Debt/GDP ratio improving.
Still elevated debt but moving toward manageable range. Fiscal reforms
ongoing with IMF support.

2000.assessment:
High oil prices boost revenues. Fiscal position improving. Debt/GDP
declining as GDP grows. Some fiscal space. Manageable deficit and debt level.

2001.assessment:
Oil revenues supporting fiscal balance. Debt/GDP declining. Manageable
fiscal position. Counterterrorism expenses adding pressure but oil
revenues compensating.

2002.assessment:
Fiscal position manageable. Oil revenues keeping budget viable. Debt
moderate. Security expenses rising. Still within manageable range.

2003.assessment:
Oil prices rising with Iraq war. Fiscal position manageable. Debt/GDP
declining. Some fiscal space developing. Manageable deficit.

2004.assessment:
High oil prices generating fiscal surpluses. First Houthi war increasing
security spending. But oil revenues more than compensating. Debt/GDP
declining. Manageable fiscal position.

2005.assessment:
Oil boom generating fiscal surpluses. Debt/GDP falling. Government
accumulating some reserves. Manageable fiscal position with declining
debt burden. Fuel subsidies expensive but affordable in boom.

2006.assessment:
High oil revenues sustaining government finances. Debt/GDP declining.
Security spending high from Houthi wars but manageable. Some fiscal
surpluses possible. Low to manageable debt.

2007.assessment:
Oil production declining structurally but prices still high. Fiscal
position holding. Security spending elevated from multiple Houthi wars.
Debt moderate. Manageable but increasingly strained.

2008.assessment:
Oil price spike helps fiscal position mid-year. Then oil crash late 2008.
Average year still positive for revenues. Fuel subsidies very expensive
at high prices. Debt moderate. Manageable fiscal position overall.

2009.assessment:
Oil price crash creates severe fiscal crisis. Oil production declining
AND prices lower. Budget deficit surges. Government begins drawing on
reserves. Sixth Houthi war military costs. Elevated debt now.

2010.assessment:
Some recovery in oil prices. Fiscal position improving but structurally
weak. Oil production decline means longer-term revenue decline. Debt
manageable but trending higher. Structural fiscal challenge.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring devastates fiscal position. AQAP attacks on oil pipeline
disrupt revenues. Government unable to pay salaries. Saudi Arabia and
Gulf donors provide emergency funds. Fiscal near-collapse.

2012.assessment:
Transitional government receiving GCC 'Friends of Yemen' pledges.
Oil revenues recovering partially. Fiscal position stabilized with
external support. Elevated debt but manageable with aid.

2013.assessment:
Fiscal position under pressure. Oil production decline accelerating.
Security spending high. Fuel subsidies taking up to 30% of budget.
Elevated debt. Moderate deficit. Structurally unsustainable.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa. Government loses control of fiscal apparatus.
Houthis remove fuel subsidies provoking protests. Budget effectively
split between two authorities. High deficit, elevated debt.

2015.assessment:
Full fiscal collapse. Civil war destroys tax and revenue infrastructure.
Oil exports cease. Government unable to pay salaries. Saudi Arabia
provides some emergency support. Unsustainable debt crisis.

2016.assessment:
Fiscal collapse complete. Two separate budget authorities (Houthi CBY-Sanaa
and government CBY-Aden). Salaries unpaid for months. Oil revenues negligible.
Budget entirely donor-dependent. Fiscal collapse condition.

2017.assessment:
Fiscal collapse continuing. CBY-Aden government unable to fund basic
services. Saudi Arabia transfers some funds but insufficient. Salary
arrears endemic. Debt beyond GDP. Fiscal collapse.

2018.assessment:
Saudi Arabia makes $2B deposit to CBY-Aden providing temporary relief.
Fiscal position marginally better but still collapsed. Unsustainable
debt/deficit position. Entirely donor-dependent.

2019.assessment:
Fiscal collapse. Saudi deposit exhausted. Government unable to pay salaries.
Rial depreciating. Unsustainable debt. Entirely aid-dependent government.
Fiscal collapse condition.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 + oil price crash compounds fiscal collapse. Currency devaluation.
Salaries of government employees (1M+) unpaid for extended periods.
Fiscal collapse. Aid the only thing keeping basic functions.

2021.assessment:
Fiscal collapse continuing. Oil prices somewhat recovered but revenues
going to Houthi areas largely. Government fiscal capacity near zero.
Total fiscal collapse.

2022.assessment:
UN truce reduces costs. Saudi transfers to CBY. Oil prices high (Ukraine).
Some fiscal improvement on government side. Still unsustainable crisis.
Houthi fiscal authority separate.

2023.assessment:
Fiscal crisis persisting. Government dependent on Saudi support. Salary
arrears continuing. Unsustainable fiscal position. Two separate fiscal
authorities. Unsustainable debt crisis.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks reduce shipping/tax revenues. US/UK strikes
add costs. Fiscal collapse continuing. Government entirely aid-dependent.
Houthi revenues from taxing Hodeidah imports. Fiscal collapse.

1996.assessment:
Low-income economy. Oil production began only 1984. GDP per capita very low
at around $540. Saleh managing oil revenues through patronage. Economy
dependent on oil (largely) and remittances.

1997.assessment:
Modest growth. Oil production near peak (around 400,000 bpd). Economy
growing slowly. Remittances from Gulf significant. Population growing
faster than economy limiting per capita gains.

1998.assessment:
Oil price crash 1998 severely impacts Yemen (heavily oil-dependent).
GDP per capita falls sharply. Budget crisis. Yemen one of poorest Arab
states. Sharp decline from 1997 levels.

1999.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Yemen economy slowly recovering from 1998 crash.
Oil production near peak. Some improvement but population growth dilutes
per capita gains.

2000.assessment:
Oil prices high. Yemen GDP near pre-1998 levels. Oil production at
approximately peak (around 450,000 bpd in 2001). Economy largely
oil-dependent with small agriculture and services sectors.

2001.assessment:
Oil production beginning to decline from peak. Post-9/11 economic
uncertainty. Yemen at moderate income level for MENA low-income category.
Remittances important supplementary income.

2002.assessment:
Oil production declining. Economy stagnating. Remittances from Gulf
disrupted post-9/11 as Yemeni workers expelled from Saudi Arabia.
Poverty widespread. GDP per capita near low-income threshold.

2003.assessment:
Oil prices rising (Iraq war). Some improvement in revenues. Oil production
continuing gradual decline but high prices compensate. Below peak but
modest recovery.

2004.assessment:
Oil revenues rising with global prices. Economy growing. First Houthi
war begins but limited economic impact. Oil production declining but
prices compensate. Near peak nominal GDP.

2005.assessment:
Oil prices surging. Revenue boom despite declining production volume.
Yemen GDP at highest level to date. Multiple Houthi wars increasing
government spending. Above peak nominal GDP.

2006.assessment:
Continued high oil prices. GDP per capita at highest level in Yemen's
history (nominal). Third Houthi war. Security spending increasing.
Above any previous peak in current USD terms.

2007.assessment:
Oil prices still high. GDP per capita rising. Oil production declining
but prices more than compensate. Pre-transition peak period. Economy
not well diversified. Remittances growing.

2008.assessment:
Oil price spike mid-2008 ($147/barrel). Yemen GDP per capita at all-time
nominal high. Then oil crash late 2008. Average for year still high.
Peak year for Yemen's GDP per capita in current USD.

2009.assessment:
Oil price crash impact. Revenue falls sharply. Sixth Houthi war.
Military spending drains budget. Economic difficulties beginning.
Below 2008 peak.

2010.assessment:
Some recovery in oil prices. GDP recovering toward 2008 peak. But oil
production declining rapidly. Structural economic deterioration beginning.
Near previous peak in nominal terms.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests paralyze economy. AQAP disrupts oil infrastructure.
GDP declines. Foreign investment flees. Political crisis suppresses
economic activity. Near peak in nominal terms but declining.

2012.assessment:
Transitional government. Some economic stabilization. Oil production
recovering after 2011 attacks. GCC and international aid flows.
Below 2008 peak but recovering.

2013.assessment:
Highest GDP per capita since transition. Oil production still declining.
Aid flows helping. Transitional government managing economy. Near peak
nominal GDP before collapse.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa. Economy begins collapse. Oil exports disrupted.
International investment suspended. GDP declining. Fuel subsidies
removed causing protests. Pre-war decline.

2015.assessment:
Full-scale civil war erupts March 2015. GDP collapses approximately 28%
in 2015. Oil exports effectively halted. Port Hodeidah blockaded.
Banking system fragmenting. Economy in free fall.

2016.assessment:
GDP continues collapse. Civil war second year. Oil exports near zero.
CBY relocated to Aden. Currency split beginning. Humanitarian crisis.
GDP ~50% below 2013 peak. Severe decline.

2017.assessment:
GDP continuing to decline. Dual currency system (Houthi/government riyal).
Hyperinflationary pressures. Coalition blockade intensified after Houthi
missile at Riyadh. Cholera epidemic. Economic catastrophe deepening.

2018.assessment:
GDP at approximately 45% of pre-war peak. Currency collapse continuing.
Rial depreciating rapidly. Government unable to pay civil servant salaries.
Severe economic collapse with collapse_below_30pct in real terms.

2019.assessment:
Some nominal stability but real economy continues collapsing.
Currency bifurcated (YER-Sanaa vs YER-Aden). Official statistics
nearly impossible to compile. Famine conditions in multiple areas.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19. Oil prices crash. GDP declines further. Currency depreciates
sharply (YER-Aden). Government completely unable to pay salaries.
Total economic collapse below 50% of pre-war level.

2021.assessment:
Economy at collapse level. Nominal GDP maybe 40-45% of 2013 peak.
Real purchasing power far lower due to currency depreciation.
Marib offensive intensification. Continued economic catastrophe.

2022.assessment:
UN truce reduces conflict costs somewhat. Oil prices surge (Ukraine war)
benefiting YE oil revenues. Some nominal improvement. But real economy
still at collapse level. GDP below 50% of peak in real terms.

2023.assessment:
Fragile ceasefire. Economy at collapsed level. UN and Saudi humanitarian
aid helps basic survival. No economic recovery. GDP per capita
approximately 45% of 2013 peak in nominal terms, far less in real terms.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks disrupt trade. US/UK strikes on Houthis.
Economy continues at collapse level. No improvement in economic
conditions. Humanitarian crisis persists.

1996.assessment:
High inflation following 1994 civil war and currency depreciation.
Post-war economic instability. Government printing money to cover
budget deficits. Inflation approximately 25%.

1997.assessment:
Inflation stabilized dramatically from post-war peaks. IMF-supported
stabilization programs. Monetary policy tightened. Inflation at moderate
level. Economic stabilization working.

1998.assessment:
Oil price crash reduces government revenues. Budget pressures lead to
some monetary financing. Inflation rises to elevated level. Fuel subsidy
reductions passed to consumers.

1999.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Fiscal pressures easing. Inflation moderate.
Currency relatively stable. Central bank maintaining some discipline.

2000.assessment:
Inflation elevated. Oil revenues higher but government spending increasing.
Fuel prices passed through. Infrastructure costs rising. Elevated inflation.

2001.assessment:
Inflation elevated. Security spending increasing. Post-9/11 economic
uncertainty. Gulf remittances disrupted. Elevated CPI from import costs.

2002.assessment:
Continued elevated inflation. Fuel price adjustments. Security spending
high. Import-dependent economy faces cost pressures. Elevated but not
high inflation.

2003.assessment:
Oil prices rising (Iraq war). Some fiscal improvement. Inflation
elevated. First Houthi conflict beginning to strain resources.

2004.assessment:
Multiple Houthi wars increase security spending. Fiscal deficit growing.
Oil revenues not sufficient to cover costs. Inflation elevated.

2005.assessment:
Oil price boom helps fiscal situation. Inflation moderate. Government
subsidizing fuel (very expensive). Some price stability from high revenues.

2006.assessment:
Fuel subsidy reductions trigger inflation. Security spending high. Elevated
inflation as government struggles to balance budget. Third Houthi war.

2007.assessment:
Inflation moderate. Food prices beginning to rise globally. Some
stabilization from high oil revenues. Moderate inflation.

2008.assessment:
Global food and fuel price spike 2008. Yemen heavily import-dependent.
Inflation surges. Fuel subsidies partially removed triggering protests.
Security spending and food price crisis simultaneously. Near high inflation.

2009.assessment:
Global commodity prices crash. Inflation drops dramatically from 2008 peak.
Deflationary pressures from global recession. Yemen inflation temporarily
low despite ongoing conflicts.

2010.assessment:
Inflation elevated again. Global food prices rising. Security spending high.
Fiscal deficit growing as oil production declining. Government borrowing
to cover gap. Elevated inflation.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring political crisis disrupts supply chains. AQAP attacks oil
pipeline multiple times. Economic paralysis. Near-high inflation from
supply disruptions and political uncertainty.

2012.assessment:
Transitional government stabilizes economy somewhat. International aid
flows. Inflation moderate. Oil production recovering. Some economic
normalization.

2013.assessment:
Elevated inflation. Transitional government struggling with fiscal pressures.
Fuel subsidy reform attempts. Security spending high. Houthi expansion
beginning. Elevated inflation.

2014.assessment:
Houthis remove fuel subsidies upon seizing Sanaa. Fuel price shock.
But overall inflation moderate for the year as oil prices fall globally.
Economy beginning destabilization.

2015.assessment:
Civil war erupts. Supply chains destroyed. Hodeidah port partially
blockaded. Currency depreciating. Inflation surges to ~22%. Near-high
inflation from conflict disruption.

2016.assessment:
Paradoxical deflationary reading due to economic collapse and currency
split. Demand destruction from poverty. Houthi-controlled areas vs
government areas have different price dynamics. National statistics nearly
impossible to compile. Data essentially meaningless.

2017.assessment:
Government rial (Aden) depreciating sharply. Civil servant salary arrears.
Cholera epidemic. Currency printing by both authorities. High inflation
in government-held areas. Data highly uncertain.

2018.assessment:
High inflation persisting from currency depreciation. Government rial
continues to weaken. Saudi deposit to CBY-Aden provides temporary relief.
High but declining inflation by year end.

2019.assessment:
Inflation somewhat moderated with CBY-Aden support. Currency still
depreciating but more slowly. Elevated inflation from ongoing conflict
and supply disruptions. Data very uncertain.

2020.assessment:
Currency collapses. Government rial loses ~50% of value in 2020.
Saudi deposit spent. Oil price crash reduces revenues. High inflation
from currency collapse. Hyperinflationary pressures in government areas.

2021.assessment:
Very high inflation from continued currency depreciation. Government rial
at fraction of pre-war value. Poverty extreme. Wages nearly worthless.
Near-hyperinflationary conditions in some areas. Very high inflation.

2022.assessment:
UN truce reduces conflict costs. Saudi oil revenues transferred to CBY.
Some currency stabilization. Inflation elevated but declining from 2021
peak. Ukraine war food price spike partially offset by aid.

2023.assessment:
Ceasefire period. Elevated inflation persisting. Currency fragile.
Humanitarian aid helps basic commodity prices. Elevated but not as extreme
as 2020-2021. Data still very uncertain.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks disrupt trade flows increasing import costs.
US/UK strikes add new conflict costs. Elevated inflation continuing.
Currency pressures. Data highly uncertain.
