1996.assessment:
Yemen moderately open economy. Oil exports dominant. Imports include food,
fuel products, manufactured goods. Trade as share of GDP elevated due to
oil export concentration. Moderate-to-open.

1997.assessment:
Trade openness high. Oil exports growing. Imports growing. Trade-to-GDP
ratio elevated reflecting oil export economy characteristics.

1998.assessment:
Oil price crash reduces export value sharply. Trade openness as percent
of GDP falls. Imports also declining as revenues fall. Moderate openness.

1999.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Trade openness improving. Yemen maintains open
trade policy. Aden free zone some activity. Open economy relative to
income level.

2000.assessment:
High oil prices boost export values. Trade openness high. Aden port
as regional hub. Open trade regime. High trade-to-GDP ratio driven
by oil exports.

2001.assessment:
Trade openness moderate-high. Post-9/11 some trade disruption.
Oil exports declining in volume but prices compensating. Aden port
activity reduced after USS Cole. Open economy overall.

2002.assessment:
Trade continues at significant level. Oil exports the driver. Imports
growing with population. Trade moderately open.

2003.assessment:
Trade openness rising with oil prices. Iraq war increases Yemen's
relative oil value. Open trade regime maintained.

2004.assessment:
High oil prices sustain trade openness. First Houthi war limited impact
on trade flows. Open economy continuing. Trade remains key to economy.

2005.assessment:
High oil prices driving high trade share of GDP. Nearly at highly_open
level due to oil export concentration. Open trade policy maintained.

2006.assessment:
High oil price era sustains trade openness. Third Houthi war limited
impact on trade statistics. Open trade regime.

2007.assessment:
Oil production declining but prices still high. Trade openness moderating.
Open economy maintained with oil export-import structure.

2008.assessment:
Oil price spike to $147 drives high trade-to-GDP ratio. Then crash.
Annual average still high. Trade openness elevated in peak year.

2009.assessment:
Oil price crash reduces trade value. Trade openness falls to moderate level.
Yemen imports still high relative to declining exports. Trade moderately open.

2010.assessment:
Some recovery in oil prices and trade. Trade openness recovering but
oil production declining structurally. Still moderately open.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring disrupts trade. AQAP attacks on oil pipeline disrupt exports.
Port activity reduced. Trade falls significantly. Moderate but declining.

2012.assessment:
Some recovery in trade after 2011 disruption. Oil pipeline repaired.
Aid inflows add to import-side of trade. Moderate trade openness.

2013.assessment:
Trade moderately open. Oil production declining limiting export value.
Imports relatively stable. Moderate trade openness pre-conflict.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa. Economy beginning to collapse. Trade disrupted.
Oil exports continue but declining. Moderate trade openness declining.

2015.assessment:
Civil war devastates trade. Hodeidah port blockade severely limits imports.
Oil exports near zero. Aden port damaged and contested. Trade collapses
to restricted level. Saudi blockade limiting humanitarian imports.

2016.assessment:
Continued trade restriction. Hodeidah partially open for humanitarian
imports. Oil exports negligible. Economy at severe contraction. Restricted
trade due to blockade and conflict.

2017.assessment:
Saudi coalition tightens blockade after Houthi missile at Riyadh
(November 2017). Hodeidah port nearly closed. Trade severely restricted.
Cholera. Economy near autarky_or_blockade.

2018.assessment:
Hodeidah battle threatening port. Stockholm Agreement December 2018
saves port from full closure. Trade severely restricted throughout.
Only humanitarian imports and some commercial flows through damaged
port infrastructure.

2019.assessment:
Trade somewhat recovered as Hodeidah port functional (albeit damaged).
UN oversight of port. Some commercial trade resuming. Still severely
restricted compared to pre-war levels.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 global trade disruption compounds Yemen's restricted trade.
Oil prices crash. Currency collapse makes imports unaffordable. Severely
restricted trade. Near blockade conditions.

2021.assessment:
Marib offensive continuing. Blockade and restrictions on Hodeidah.
Trade severely restricted. Aid imports a major component of total
imports. Restricted trade near autarky levels.

2022.assessment:
UN truce improves Hodeidah access. Oil prices high. Some trade recovery.
Saudi CBY deposit enables more imports. Trade restricted but improving.
Still far below pre-war levels.

2023.assessment:
Ceasefire period. Trade somewhat stabilized at restricted level. Hodeidah
port operational. Oil exports minimal. Imports via Aden and Hodeidah.
Still restricted compared to pre-war.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks disrupt global shipping and Yemen's own imports
through Hodeidah. US/UK strikes add insecurity. Trade deteriorating from
2022-2023 improvements. Restricted to near-blockade conditions.

1996.assessment:
High unemployment in post-civil war Yemen. Large informal sector means
official figures understate joblessness. Many employed in low-productivity
subsistence agriculture. Youth unemployment especially high.

1997.assessment:
High unemployment. Limited formal economy. Oil sector employs few directly.
Agriculture and informal trade dominant. Returning migrants from Saudi Arabia
add to labor supply.

1998.assessment:
Oil price crash reduces government employment. Informal sector absorbs
some workers but youth unemployment particularly high. Elevated unemployment.

1999.assessment:
Persistently high unemployment. Large youth cohort entering labor market.
Economy not creating sufficient jobs. Informal sector provides survival
but not meaningful employment.

2000.assessment:
High unemployment broadly stable. Oil revenues not generating broad employment.
Agriculture majority of employment but low productivity. Urban youth
unemployment very high.

2001.assessment:
Gulf remittances reduced post-9/11 as Yemenis expelled from Saudi Arabia.
Unemployment rising. Public sector hiring constrained. High unemployment.

2002.assessment:
Unemployment rising. Limited private sector investment. Security situation
deterring foreign investment. Youth bulge straining labor market.

2003.assessment:
High unemployment. Oil production declining. Security challenges deterring
investment. Youth unemployment reportedly 30%+. High and rising unemployment.

2004.assessment:
High unemployment. First Houthi war adds security premium discouraging
investment. Large youth population entering labor market. High unemployment.

2005.assessment:
High unemployment persisting. Some improvement in oil revenues but not
generating employment. Construction and services growing slightly.
Still high unemployment rate.

2006.assessment:
High unemployment. Third Houthi war. Some job creation from oil revenue
boom but insufficient for growing labor force. High unemployment persists.

2007.assessment:
Unemployment remains high. Economy not diversified. Multiple Houthi wars
deterring private sector. Youth unemployment very high (estimated 35%+).

2008.assessment:
High unemployment. Global food crisis makes situation worse for poor.
Security challenges increasing. Youth unemployment crisis. High unemployment.

2009.assessment:
High unemployment. Oil production falling rapidly. Security spending crowding
out development. Sixth Houthi war in full force. Youth unemployment approaching
crisis levels.

2010.assessment:
Official rate slightly lower but underlying conditions unchanged. AQAP
activity deterring business. Southern Movement protests include workers
demanding employment. High unemployment de facto.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring crisis destroys jobs. Tourism collapses. FDI exits. Private
sector freezes. Government unable to pay wages. Very high unemployment
driven by political crisis.

2012.assessment:
High unemployment persisting into transitional period. International aid
creates some jobs in humanitarian sector. Private sector cautious.
Unemployment remains very high.

2013.assessment:
High unemployment. National Dialogue Conference ongoing. No structural
economic reforms. Youth bulge still entering labor market. High and
persistent unemployment.

2014.assessment:
Rising unemployment as Houthis seize Sanaa and economy destabilizes.
Private sector contracting. Public sector salary delays. Unemployment
rising. Very high.

2015.assessment:
Civil war destroys economy. Private sector collapses. Public sector
workers not paid for months. Unemployment surges to crisis levels.
Over 25% formal unemployment; actual much higher including informally
displaced workers.

2016.assessment:
Catastrophically high unemployment. Civil war economy. Private sector
destroyed. Government unable to pay salaries for months at a time.
Crisis unemployment levels with no recovery in sight.

2017.assessment:
Continued crisis unemployment. Cholera epidemic removes workers from
labor force. Government salary arrears of 8+ months. Crisis employment.

2018.assessment:
Persistent crisis unemployment. Some humanitarian economy creating jobs.
Private economy largely destroyed in conflict areas. Very high unemployment.

2019.assessment:
Crisis unemployment persisting. STC seizes Aden disrupting south's economy.
Humanitarian sector dominant employer. Formal economy destroyed. Very high
unemployment across all territories.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 adds to unemployment. Humanitarian economy the main employer.
Private sector collapsed. Subsistence agriculture survival for rural
population. Crisis unemployment.

2021.assessment:
Crisis unemployment continues. Marib offensive destroys more livelihoods.
Millions dependent on aid for survival. Formal labor market essentially
non-functional. Crisis gt_40pct in effective terms.

2022.assessment:
UN truce reduces violence but does not create jobs. Unemployment remains
at crisis levels. Some humanitarian economy recovery. No structural change.

2023.assessment:
Crisis unemployment continuing. Ceasefire period. Humanitarian aid dominant
economic activity. Private sector remains suppressed. Very high unemployment.

2024.assessment:
Crisis unemployment persisting. Houthi Red Sea attacks add economic disruption.
US/UK strikes add insecurity. No economic recovery or job creation. Very high
unemployment across all territories.

1996.assessment:
Full international recognition. UN member, Arab League member, OIC member.
Relations normalized with Saudi Arabia after 1994 civil war and 1995
border agreement. Active diplomatic relations with major powers.

1997.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Active embassies in/from major states.
Parliamentary elections internationally observed. Normal relations.

1998.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Some Western concerns about terrorism
not yet affecting diplomatic standing. Normal relations.

1999.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Saleh's Yemen actively engaged
with international community. Normal diplomatic relations.

2000.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. USS Cole bombing causes some concern but
Saleh cooperating with US. Saudi-Yemen border agreement 2000 improves
regional relations. Normal diplomatic standing.

2001.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Post-9/11 Yemen positions itself as
counterterrorism partner. US-Yemen security cooperation elevated.
Normal diplomatic relations. Active engagement.

2002.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. First US drone strike in Yemen causes some
friction but relationship maintained. Yemen counterterrorism partner.

2003.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Yemen opposes Iraq war but maintains
normal relations with US/West. Active UN participation. Normal relations.

2004.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations. First Houthi war internal. Counterterrorism
cooperation with US. GCC relations important. Active international engagement.

2005.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations maintained despite multiple internal conflicts.
Yemen-Saudi relations strained somewhat by Houthi issue. US counterterrorism
partnership active.

2006.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Competitive elections internationally
observed and praised. Yemen active in international forums. Good diplomatic
standing despite governance challenges.

2007.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations. Some concerns from GCC about instability.
Counterterrorism partnership maintained. Partial concerns growing.

2008.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations. US Embassy bombing causes friction.
Counterterrorism partnership stressed but maintained. Some Western
embassies increase security. Normal with minor concerns.

2009.assessment:
Diplomatic relations maintained but AQAP prominence raises serious
concerns internationally. US increasing pressure on Yemen. Saudi Arabia
intervening in north. Partial isolation concerns growing.

2010.assessment:
Some Western embassies closed temporarily after AQAP bomb plot targeting
aircraft (October 2010). Most embassies operational. Normal with some
temporary closures from security concerns.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring period. Multiple embassies close temporarily for security.
International community supporting GCC transition process. Political
isolation concerns but Yemen state still recognized. Partial isolation.

2012.assessment:
Transitional government internationally recognized and supported.
GCC and Western governments active in supporting transition. Full
diplomatic support for transition process. Active international engagement.

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. National Dialogue Conference internationally
supported. 'Friends of Yemen' group active. Some embassies reducing
staff due to security but diplomatic relations normal.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa. Embassies concerned but operating. Hadi government
still internationally recognized. International community calling for
political solution. Partial closures beginning.

2015.assessment:
Most embassies in Sanaa close February-March 2015. Hadi government
recognized internationally from Riyadh. Saudi-led coalition operation
internationally legitimized (Hadi request). Significant isolation of
country but government recognized in exile.

2016.assessment:
Hadi government recognized by international community from Riyadh/Aden.
No embassies in Sanaa. International community engaged in peace process.
Yemen isolated from normal diplomatic functioning. Significant isolation
of the country while government recognized in exile.

2017.assessment:
Continued significant isolation. No foreign embassies in Sanaa.
Hadi government recognized from Riyadh. Peace talks stalled.
Houthis internationally isolated. Significant isolation of country.

2018.assessment:
Significant isolation continues. Stockholm Agreement brings UN
engagement. Hadi government recognized internationally. No normal
embassy function in Sanaa. Yemen highly isolated.

2019.assessment:
Significant isolation. STC seizes Aden complicating recognized government.
No foreign embassies operational in Yemen. Diplomatic engagement through
Riyadh. Yemen highly isolated from normal international relations.

2020.assessment:
Continued significant isolation. Pandemic reduces diplomatic activity.
Yemen government operates from Riyadh/Aden. No normal diplomatic
environment. Significant isolation.

2021.assessment:
Significant isolation. Biden administration removes Houthi FTO designation
to facilitate humanitarian access. No improvement in diplomatic access.
Yemen highly isolated from normal international relations.

2022.assessment:
UN truce creates diplomatic space. PLC formed with broader international
recognition. Saudi-Houthi negotiations through Omani mediation. Some
improvement from 2015-2021 isolation but still significantly isolated.

2023.assessment:
Saudi-Iran rapprochement (March 2023) brings hope for Yemen peace.
Saudi-Houthi direct talks. China-brokered Saudi-Iran deal changes
regional dynamics. Some diplomatic opening but country still significantly
isolated from normal international relations.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks re-internationalizes Yemen crisis negatively.
US/UK airstrikes on Houthis. Diplomatic hopes from 2023 setback.
Some coalition countries reviewing Yemen engagement. Significant isolation
continuing with new international conflict dimension.

2025.assessment:
Continued significant isolation. US/UK strikes ongoing. Houthis re-designated
FTO. No normal diplomatic functioning. Yemen highly isolated with multiple
external military actors involved.

1996.assessment:
Low FDI. Yemen attracting some oil sector investment (Hunt Oil, TotalEnergies).
Political risk and governance concerns limiting non-oil FDI. Very low to
minimal FDI outside oil sector.

1997.assessment:
Low FDI. Oil sector attracting some investment. Political stability
allowing modest confidence. Very low to minimal overall FDI.

1998.assessment:
FDI declines with oil price crash. Investors less interested in oil
exploration. Security concerns from extremism beginning. Minimal FDI.

1999.assessment:
Low FDI. Some oil sector investment returning with higher prices.
Non-oil FDI minimal. Very low overall.

2000.assessment:
Low FDI. USS Cole bombing deters non-oil investment. Oil sector
attracting limited exploration investment. Very low.

2001.assessment:
Low FDI. Post-9/11 counterterrorism branding may deter some investors.
Oil sector and Aden free zone attracting limited investment. Very low.

2002.assessment:
Limburg attack further deters investment. FDI remaining low.
Only oil sector willing to invest despite risks.

2003.assessment:
Low FDI. Oil prices rising. Some oil sector investment. First Houthi
war discourages broader investment. Very low FDI.

2004.assessment:
FDI somewhat higher. Oil sector investment. Tourism sector showing
some growth. Low overall but slightly above minimal. Low FDI.

2005.assessment:
Low to moderate FDI. Oil price boom attracting oil sector investment.
Some Aden port/container investment. GCC investors showing some interest.
Low but rising FDI.

2006.assessment:
FDI relatively higher in peak oil era. GCC investors in real estate
and tourism. Oil sector exploration. Low to moderate FDI at peak period.

2007.assessment:
Low FDI. Multiple Houthi wars deterring investment. Security challenges
growing. Oil investment continues but non-oil remains very low.

2008.assessment:
FDI low. US Embassy bombing August 2008 sharply reduces investor confidence.
Oil sector investment declining as reserves deplete. Low FDI.

2009.assessment:
FDI drops sharply. AQAP establishment, sixth Houthi war, security crisis
all deter investment. Oil sector investment declining with reserve depletion.
Very low FDI.

2010.assessment:
FDI minimal. Security risks from AQAP deterring all but oil investment.
Oil production structurally declining reducing oil FDI. Minimal FDI.

2011.assessment:
FDI collapses with Arab Spring. Political crisis scares all investors.
Oil companies evacuate staff. Minimal FDI. Essentially zero new investment.

2012.assessment:
Minimal FDI. Transitional government attracting some interest but security
risks deter investment. Oil sector minimal. Minimal FDI.

2013.assessment:
Very low FDI. Some oil sector interest. National Dialogue Conference
reduces political risk somewhat. Still very low/minimal FDI.

2014.assessment:
FDI near zero. Houthis seizing Sanaa scares investors. Oil companies
beginning to evacuate. Minimal-to-negative FDI as investors withdraw.

2015.assessment:
Negative FDI (net outflows). Civil war begins. All major investors
withdraw. Oil companies suspend operations. Capital flight. Negative
net FDI flows.

2016.assessment:
Negative or near-zero FDI. Civil war. No new investment. Previous
investors withdrawing remaining assets. Negative FDI.

2017.assessment:
Near-zero to negative FDI. War economy. No investment possible.
Negative or minimal net FDI.

2018.assessment:
Negative FDI. War economy. No new investment. All foreign investment
suspended indefinitely.

2019.assessment:
Negative FDI. Ongoing civil war. No investment possible. Negative net
flows as remaining investors extract capital.

2020.assessment:
Near-zero FDI. Civil war continuing. No meaningful investment possible.
Minimal or negative net flows.

2021.assessment:
Negligible FDI. War economy. No investment environment.
Negative to zero net FDI flows.

2022.assessment:
UN truce creates slight improvement in business confidence. Minimal
new investment. Still essentially no FDI environment. Minimal.

2023.assessment:
Minimal FDI. Ceasefire period. Peace talks raising slight hopes. No
meaningful investment in war economy. Minimal FDI.

2024.assessment:
Negative FDI. Houthi Red Sea attacks disrupt business. US/UK strikes
increase uncertainty. No investment possible. Negative net flows.

1996.assessment:
No significant foreign military presence. Yemen sovereign state with
Saleh controlling unified military. No foreign bases or troops.
Full sovereignty.

1997.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Military entirely
under Yemeni state control.

1998.assessment: No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty maintained.

1999.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Saleh's unified Yemen fully sovereign.
US military cooperation discussed but no presence.

2000.assessment:
USS Cole bombing leads to US naval and FBI presence for investigation.
Small US advisory/training presence. Yemen-US counterterrorism cooperation
beginning. Limited invited advisors.

2001.assessment:
US counterterrorism advisors in Yemen post-9/11. Limited US military
training presence. Yemen invited cooperation. Limited invited advisors.

2002.assessment:
US CIA drone operations in Yemen (first strike November 2002). US
Special Forces advisors training Yemen military. Limited but significant
US presence invited by Saleh government. Limited invited advisors.

2003.assessment:
US counterterrorism advisors and drone operations continuing. Limited
invited foreign military presence. Sovereignty maintained with invited
assistance.

2004.assessment:
US counterterrorism advisors. First Houthi war with no foreign involvement.
Yemen government managing war without foreign military assistance.
Limited US advisory presence.

2005.assessment:
Limited US counterterrorism presence. Multiple Houthi wars managed
by Yemen military alone. Drone operations occasional. Limited invited
advisors.

2006.assessment:
US counterterrorism advisory presence. Drone operations ongoing.
Yemen managing Houthi wars and AQAP independently. Limited invited
advisors.

2007.assessment:
US counterterrorism presence. Drone strikes on AQAP. Yemen managing
fourth Houthi war without foreign help. Limited invited advisors.

2008.assessment:
US counterterrorism advisors. Drone operations increasing. Fifth Houthi
war managed domestically. US strike kills AQAP members. Limited invited
advisors.

2009.assessment:
Saudi Arabia launches Operation Scorched Earth against Houthis in north
Yemen (crossing border). Significant Saudi military operation on Yemen
territory at government invitation. Saudi ground and air operations
in Saada. Significant invited forces.

2010.assessment:
Saudi ceasefire with Houthis but Saudi border area remains militarized.
US drone operations increasing in scope and frequency. Limited-to-significant
invited foreign forces.

2011.assessment:
US drone operations intensifying against AQAP. Saleh injured in coup
attempt; treated in Saudi Arabia. GCC diplomatic intervention managing
transition. Limited US military and GCC diplomatic presence.

2012.assessment:
US drone operations frequent against AQAP. US Special Forces advisors
training Yemeni troops. GCC supporting transition. Limited invited foreign
military forces.

2013.assessment:
US drone operations continuing against AQAP. US Special Forces advisors
present. Limited invited foreign military presence.

2014.assessment:
US drone operations ongoing. US evacuates Special Forces advisors as
Houthis advance September 2014. Houthi seize Sanaa. Military situation
deteriorating. Limited invited forces then evacuated.

2015.assessment:
Saudi-led coalition begins Operation Decisive Storm (March 26, 2015).
Coalition includes Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt, Jordan, Sudan, Bahrain,
Kuwait, Morocco, Senegal. Air, naval, and ground operations at scale.
Iran supporting Houthis. Massive foreign military presence.
Active foreign combat operations.

2016.assessment:
Saudi-led coalition active combat operations continuing. Coalition air
strikes daily. UAE ground forces operating. Sudanese ground troops in
Yemen. Iranian advisors supporting Houthis. Active foreign combat operations.

2017.assessment:
Coalition air operations continuing. UAE ground forces operating in south
(building STC). Iranian missile and drone support to Houthis (used against
Saudi Arabia). US limited operations against AQAP resuming.
Active foreign combat operations.

2018.assessment:
Coalition battle for Hodeidah (June-December 2018). Intense air operations.
UAE ground forces in south. Saudi ground forces at northern border.
Stockholm Agreement December 2018. Active foreign combat operations.

2019.assessment:
Coalition operations continuing but reduced post-Stockholm. UAE draws down
ground forces. Saudi air operations continuing. Iranian support to Houthis
with drones/missiles used against Saudi Aramco. Contested mixed presence.

2020.assessment:
Saudi air operations continuing. UAE reduced presence. Iranian support
to Houthis ongoing. Multiple foreign military actors. Contested mixed
presence with significant invited forces from Saudi-side.

2021.assessment:
Saudi air operations continuing intensively (Marib area). Houthi attacks
on Saudi territory. Iranian drone/missile support to Houthis. UAE building
STC forces. US SOCOM operations against AQAP. Multiple foreign militaries.
Significant invited forces and active combat operations.

2022.assessment:
UN truce April-October 2022 reduces Saudi coalition operations. Saudi
air strikes stop during truce. Iranian support to Houthis continues.
UAE-backed STC forces. Post-truce operations reduced. Significant invited
forces but reduced active combat operations.

2023.assessment:
Saudi air operations minimal (ceasefire). Saudi-Houthi direct negotiations.
Iranian support to Houthis including maritime capability. UAE-backed STC.
Significantly reduced but still significant foreign military presence.

2024.assessment:
US/UK begin strikes on Yemen (Operation Prosperity Guardian/Poseidon Archer)
from January 2024 responding to Houthi Red Sea attacks. Saudi coalition
reduced but continuing. Iranian military support to Houthis with maritime
capability. Multiple uninvited and invited foreign military actors.
Active foreign combat operations.

2025.assessment:
US/UK operations continuing. Houthi Red Sea attacks continuing.
Multiple foreign militaries active. Active foreign combat operations
with multiple uninvited actors. Contested mixed presence.

1996.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Post-1994 civil war mostly resolved. Some Somali
refugees entering Yemen (as receiving country). Minimal outward displacement.
Some returns of displaced from 1994 civil war.

1997.assessment:
Stable. Minimal displacement. Yemen primarily a destination country
for Somali and Ethiopian refugees. Minimal outward flows.

1998.assessment:
Stable. Yemen continuing to receive Somali and Ethiopian migrants.
Minimal domestic displacement. Stable minimal flows.

1999.assessment:
Stable. Minimal displacement. Economy poor but security broadly stable.
Minimal outward displacement.

2000.assessment:
Minimal displacement. USS Cole bombing and security concerns. Some
internal movement. Still stable minimal new displacement.

2001.assessment:
Stable. Post-9/11 Yemeni expulsions from Saudi Arabia create internal
returnee flow rather than outward displacement. Minimal new displacement.

2002.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Security challenges but no mass displacement
events. Stable minimal.

2003.assessment:
Minimal displacement. AQAP activity in some areas but no mass displacement.
Stable Yemen population largely in place.

2004.assessment:
Moderate displacement from first Houthi war in Saada. Internal displacement
of Saada population. Moderate outflows at low level. Conflict displacement
beginning.

2005.assessment:
Moderate displacement from second Houthi war. Saada fighting displaces
more civilians. Multiple Houthi wars creating persistent displacement
in northern Yemen.

2006.assessment:
Moderate displacement. Third Houthi war. Saada and surrounding areas
experiencing civilian displacement. Moderate outflows below 1% of population.

2007.assessment:
Fourth Houthi war increases displacement. Multiple conflict areas.
Southern Movement displacement. OCHA estimates 100,000+ displaced in
north by fourth war. Growing but still below 1% of population.

2008.assessment:
Fifth Houthi war. Increasing displacement. Security issues in south
with Southern Movement. Moderate outflows approaching 1%.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Houthi war with Saudi intervention. OCHA estimates 175,000+
displaced in Saada in 2009 alone. Crossing into significant outflows.
Saudi aerial bombardment displaces entire communities.

2010.assessment:
Post-war ceasefire. Some IDPs returning to Saada. But AQAP activity
displacing civilians in south. Net displacement moderate. Below 1% of population.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests and violence. AQAP seizes Zinjibar displacing
Abyan population. Political crisis produces new displacement. Around
500,000 IDPs estimated by end of 2011. Significant outflows ~1%.

2012.assessment:
Some IDPs returning as AQAP pushed out of Zinjibar. But overall IDP
population remains high at ~400,000+. Moderate outflows continuing.

2013.assessment:
Persistent displacement. AQAP activity displacing civilians in south.
Houthi expansion displacing communities in north. IDP population growing
to ~300,000+. Moderate outflows.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seizing Sanaa and advancing south displacing hundreds of thousands.
IDP population jumps to ~600,000+ by end of 2014. Significant outflows
crossing 1% of population.

2015.assessment:
Civil war catastrophic displacement. Coalition airstrikes and ground
combat displace approximately 2.5 million people in 2015 alone. IDP
total reaches ~3 million. Approximately 10% of population displaced
in a single year. Catastrophic.

2016.assessment:
Continued mass displacement. New displacement of ~1 million in 2016.
Total IDP population approximately 3 million. Mass displacement
3-10% level annually. Catastrophic humanitarian situation.

2017.assessment:
Continued mass displacement. Total IDPs approximately 3.5 million.
Cholera epidemic affecting mainly displaced populations. New displacement
from front line movements. Mass displacement level.

2018.assessment:
Continued mass displacement. Hodeidah battle displaces hundreds of thousands.
Total IDPs approximately 3.5-4 million. Mass displacement level.
World's worst humanitarian crisis per UN.

2019.assessment:
Total IDPs approximately 3.6 million. Some returns in Hodeidah area
post-Stockholm Agreement. New displacement from south (STC-IRG fighting).
Mass displacement continuing. Cumulative ~250,000 dead including indirect.

2020.assessment:
Total IDPs reach 4+ million. Houthi Marib offensive creates new
displacement. COVID-19. Currency collapse forcing economic displacement.
Mass displacement at 3-10% level annually.

2021.assessment:
IDP total reaches approximately 4.3 million. Marib offensive most intense
since 2015. New displacement from front line areas. Mass displacement
continues. UN estimates 21.1 million people in need of humanitarian aid.

2022.assessment:
UN truce reduces new displacement. Some IDPs returning. Total IDP count
still approximately 4.3 million. New displacement lower than peak years.
Significant but reduced from 2021. Significant outflows.

2023.assessment:
Fragile ceasefire. Some IDPs returning. New displacement from residual
conflict. Total IDPs approximately 4+ million. Significant outflows at
lower level than peak conflict years.

2024.assessment:
Houthi Red Sea attacks. US/UK strikes on Yemen. Some new displacement
from US/UK strikes in Houthi-controlled areas. Total IDPs still 4M+.
Significant outflows continuing.

1996.assessment:
No sanctions on Yemen. Country integrated into international community.
Member of Arab League, UN, OIC. No restrictions from international bodies.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC; UN Security Council

1997.assessment: No sanctions on Yemen.

1998.assessment:
No sanctions on Yemen as a state. Some individual al-Qaeda-linked
Yemenis on UN terror lists.

1999.assessment: No sanctions on Yemen state.

2000.assessment:
USS Cole bombing but no state sanctions on Yemen. Some individual
designations of al-Qaeda members. Yemen cooperating with US on
counterterrorism. No state sanctions.

2001.assessment:
No state sanctions. Individual al-Qaeda member designations. Yemen
cooperating with US counterterrorism post-9/11. US considers Yemen
a partner not a target for sanctions.

2002.assessment:
No state sanctions. Individual AQAP designations. US drone strike
kills AQAP leader but no sanctions on Yemen state.

2003.assessment:
No state sanctions. Individual designations of AQAP members.
Yemen is counterterrorism partner.

2004.assessment:
No state sanctions. First Houthi war is internal. Individual AQAP
designations continuing. Yemen state not sanctioned.

2005.assessment:
No state sanctions. Multiple Houthi wars internal. AQAP member designations.
Yemen remains counterterrorism partner.

2006.assessment:
No state sanctions. AQAP designations ongoing. Yemen cooperating
with international community despite internal conflicts.

2007.assessment:
No state sanctions. Yemen state remains internationally recognized
partner. Individual AQAP designations.

2008.assessment:
No state sanctions. US Embassy bombing but Yemen government not blamed.
Individual designations. No state-level sanctions.

2009.assessment:
No state sanctions. AQAP formally established. US designates AQAP as
Foreign Terrorist Organization but does not sanction Yemen state.
Counterterrorism partnership maintained.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC; UN Security Council

2010.assessment:
No state sanctions. AQAP individual designations increasing.
Yemen remains counterterrorism partner despite governance failures.

2011.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 2014 (October 2011) imposes targeted sanctions against
individuals threatening peace. GCC deal facilitated by international
community. No comprehensive state sanctions. Targeted individuals only.

2012.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions under UNSC 2014. Transitional government
internationally recognized. No state sanctions. International community
supporting transition.

2013.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions under UNSC framework. No state sanctions.
Yemen government internationally supported.

2014.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 2140 (February 2014) establishes targeted sanctions
against Houthi leaders and those threatening Yemen peace. Houthi
leaders Abdulmalik al-Houthi and others designated. Targeted only.

2015.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 2216 (April 2015) imposes arms embargo on Houthis/Saleh
and targeted financial sanctions/travel bans. Saudi-led coalition is
internationally recognized operation at Hadi government's request.
Targeted individual and Houthi-entity sanctions only, not state-wide.

2016.assessment:
Continued targeted sanctions under UNSC 2216 regime. Houthi and
Saleh-aligned individuals designated. No comprehensive state sanctions.
Yemen government receiving international aid.

2017.assessment:
Continued UNSC targeted sanctions on Houthis/Saleh network. US Treasury
designates additional Houthi leaders. Coalition blockade tightened after
Houthi missile attack on Riyadh but not formal comprehensive sanctions.

2018.assessment:
Continued targeted individual sanctions on Houthi leaders. US designates
Houthi arms procurement networks. UNSC Panel reports on violations.
No comprehensive state sanctions.

2019.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Houthi leaders continuing. US Treasury
sanctions Houthi financial networks. No comprehensive state sanctions.

2020.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Houthi leaders. Houthi arms networks
sanctioned by US and EU. No comprehensive state sanctions on Yemen.

2021.assessment:
Biden administration removes Houthi FTO designation (February 2021)
to facilitate humanitarian access. Targeted individual sanctions
maintained under UNSC framework. No comprehensive state sanctions.

2022.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Houthi leaders maintained. UNSC 2216
arms embargo on Houthis. No comprehensive state sanctions. UN truce period.

2023.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Houthi leaders. Saudi-Iran rapprochement
reduces geopolitical pressure. No comprehensive state sanctions. Targeted only.

2024.assessment:
Biden administration re-designates Houthis as Specially Designated Global
Terrorist (SDGT) January 2024 due to Red Sea attacks. Not full FTO.
Additional targeted sanctions on Houthi leadership. US/UK airstrikes
on Yemen from January 2024. Targeted individual sanctions regime.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC; US State Department

2025.assessment:
Trump administration re-designates Houthis as FTO January 2025.
Additional targeted financial sanctions. US/UK strikes continuing.
No comprehensive state sanctions on Yemen. Targeted individual/entity sanctions.

1996.assessment:
Freedom House CL=6. Authoritarian state with significant restrictions.
Limited press freedom. Political prisoners. Arbitrary detention. Some
opposition parties and civil society but heavily constrained. Tribal
customary law operates parallel to formal system.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1996

1997.assessment:
FH CL=6. Parliamentary elections increase nominal political participation
but civil liberties remain severely restricted. Press censorship. State
security surveillance. Political detentions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1997

1998.assessment:
FH CL=6. Severe civil liberties restrictions persist. Political opposition
tolerated in limited form. Islamist groups operating within constraints.
Women face legal and social discrimination. Forced disappearances reported.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1998

1999.assessment:
FH CL=6. Presidential election held but non-competitive. Civil society
constrained. Press subject to government pressure. Religious freedom
restricted for non-Muslims. Human rights defenders targeted.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1999

2000.assessment:
FH CL=5. Some slight improvement in formal civil society space but security
crackdowns post-USS Cole. Significant restrictions remain. Freedom of
assembly curtailed. Press restricted but some independent media exists.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2000

2001.assessment:
FH CL=5. Post-9/11 counterterrorism crackdown limits civil liberties.
Security detentions increase. Press restricted. Constitutional referendum
passed limiting term limits. Some political space but significant restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2001

2002.assessment:
FH CL=5. Counterterrorism cooperation with US has mixed civil liberties
impact. Some detentions without trial. Press restricted but civil society
more active. Local council elections provide some participation.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2002

2003.assessment:
FH CL=5. Political opposition tolerated within limits. Press faces harassment.
Security services detain suspects without due process. Some independent
civil society. Significant but not the most severe restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2003

2004.assessment:
FH CL=5. Houthi war begins June 2004, bringing military crackdowns in
Saada. Houthi sympathizers arrested. Journalists arrested. Security
concerns used to justify civil liberties restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2004

2005.assessment:
FH CL=5. Multiple Houthi wars increasing security detentions. Southern
protests suppressed. Political opposition increasingly restricted as
state faces multiple security challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
FH CL=5. Presidential election most competitive in Yemen's history.
Some political opening but sustained civil liberties restrictions.
Third Houthi war. Southern Movement activists arrested.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006

2007.assessment:
FH CL=5. Southern Movement activists detained. Fourth Houthi war brings
more restrictions in north. Journalists jailed. Civil liberties declining
as state faces mounting challenges. Significant restrictions throughout.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2007

2008.assessment:
FH CL=6. Fifth Houthi war and US Embassy bombing trigger security crackdowns.
Southern Movement protests suppressed with violence. Journalists imprisoned.
Deteriorating civil liberties as security pressures mount.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2008

2009.assessment:
FH CL=6. Sixth Houthi war. Saudi intervention. AQAP activity. Comprehensive
crackdowns on all opposition. Southern Movement leaders jailed. Parliament
elections postponed. Multiple civil liberties violations.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2009

2010.assessment:
FH CL=6. Southern activists jailed. Journalists arrested. Drone strikes
against AQAP without due process. Parliamentary elections postponed.
Declining civic space. Severe civil liberties restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010

2011.assessment:
FH CL=6. Arab Spring protests met with violent repression (March massacre
in Sanaa, >50 protesters killed). GCC deal provides partial immunity for
Saleh but no civil liberties improvement. AQAP seizes Abyan.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011

2012.assessment:
FH CL=6. Hadi transitional government brings some improvement in political
space but civil liberties fundamentally unchanged. National Dialogue
Conference preparations include civil society. Drone strikes on AQAP
continue without accountability.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012

2013.assessment:
FH CL=6. National Dialogue Conference includes broader participation.
Some improvement in formal political rights. Civil liberties still severely
restricted. Houthi intimidation in north. AQAP controls Abyan areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013

2014.assessment:
FH CL=6. Houthis seize Sanaa September 2014. Houthi forces impose
restrictions in areas under their control. Kidnapping of journalists
and civil society figures. Effectively two different civil liberties
regimes emerging (government areas vs Houthi areas).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014

2015.assessment:
FH CL=7 (worst category). Full-scale civil war effectively ends
meaningful civil liberties. Houthi areas: forced conscription, arbitrary
detention, media censorship. Coalition-controlled areas: arbitrary detention,
drone/airstrikes killing civilians. No effective civil liberties framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015

2016.assessment:
FH CL=7. Civil war continues. Houthis detain journalists, human rights
defenders, political opponents. Coalition airstrikes on civilians.
Multiple actors violating international humanitarian law. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016

2017.assessment:
FH CL=7. Cholera epidemic devastates population with no civil protections.
Houthi media control complete in north. UAE-backed forces operate detention
facilities in south with reported torture. No civil liberties framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017

2018.assessment:
FH CL=7. Continued total collapse of civil liberties. Houthi control
of media, arbitrary detentions, forced conscription including children.
Coalition/UAE secret detention facilities documented. Mass civilian
displacement. No civil liberties in any territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018

2019.assessment:
FH CL=7. Persistent total civil liberties collapse. STC-IRG fighting
adds more actors violating rights in south. Houthis continue systematic
repression in northwest. Famine conditions. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019

2020.assessment:
FH CL=7. COVID-19 compounds suffering with no public health rights.
Houthi repression unabated. Coalition airstrikes continuing. No improvement
in civil liberties. Total collapse continues.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020

2021.assessment:
FH CL=7. Houthi Marib offensive brings more displacement and repression.
Coalition strikes on civilians continue. No civil liberties framework
functional in any territory. Famine and disease kill more than combat.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021

2022.assessment:
FH CL=7. UN truce reduces kinetic violence but civil liberties not improved.
Houthi repression of dissent continues. Coalition areas: arbitrary detention
by various factions. No civil liberties. Truce does not address rights.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2022

2023.assessment:
FH CL=7. Fragile ceasefire does not restore civil liberties. Houthis
detain journalists and civil society. Continued violations across all
areas. No civil liberties framework. Worst possible rating.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2023

2024.assessment:
FH CL=7. US/UK strikes on Houthis add more violence without improving
civil liberties. Houthi-controlled areas remain most repressive. All
factions continue violations. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
FH CL=7 expected. Ongoing conflict with no civil liberties framework.
Houthi repression, coalition violations, STC abuses all persist.
No change from previous years expected.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2025

1996.assessment:
Saleh manages ruling coalition through patronage. GPC and military elites
broadly aligned. Islamist Islah party (Hashid tribal confederation) in
formal coalition with GPC. Significant competition managed through
patronage networks. Stable authoritarian coalition.

1997.assessment:
GPC wins parliamentary elections 1997 consolidating Saleh's base. Islah
in opposition but within system. Military elites loyal. Tribal leaders
co-opted. Competition managed through patronage. Functional stability.

1998.assessment:
Saleh consolidates power. Military loyalty maintained through promotions
and patronage. Tribal networks tied to government through financial transfers.
Elite cohesion solid with Saleh managing tensions.

1999.assessment:
Presidential election 1999 (Saleh wins 96%). No serious elite challenge.
Patronage system working. Military and tribal elites satisfied.
Managed competition within unified governance framework.

2000.assessment:
Security pressures post-USS Cole challenge Saleh's management capacity.
Military and security elites jockeying for influence in counterterrorism
operations. Some tensions but Saleh manages through patronage.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 US engagement increases Saleh's external legitimacy but
complicates domestic elite management. Military factions competing for
counterterrorism resources. Managed competition continuing.

2002.assessment:
Saleh managing elite factions through US counterterrorism support and oil
revenues. Military and tribal patronage intact. Islah partly competing
partly cooperating. Broadly managed competition.

2003.assessment:
Saleh's coalition managing but showing signs of strain. Oil revenues
declining. Military loyalty maintained but costlier. Southern grievances
beginning to penetrate elite level. Managed competition.

2004.assessment:
First Houthi war strains elite unity. Saleh accused by some elites of
mishandling war. General Ali Mohsen al-Ahmar (Saleh's kinsman) leads
military campaign but tension with Saleh growing. Managed competition
fraying at edges.

2005.assessment:
Multiple Houthi wars straining Saleh's management of military elites.
Competition between Saleh family faction and other military families
becoming more visible. Revenues declining. Still managed but increasingly
open competition emerging.

2006.assessment:
Presidential election 2006 most competitive ever. Faisal bin Shamlan
(with Islah and others) wins 22% - signals elite willingness to challenge.
Third Houthi war. Southern movement growing. Elite cohesion fracturing
but Saleh still dominant.

2007.assessment:
Fourth Houthi war. Southern Movement among retired military officers.
Saleh managing military leadership but tensions with Ali Mohsen visible.
Oil revenues dropping. Patronage system straining. Open factional conflict
within elite.

2008.assessment:
Fifth Houthi war and US Embassy bombing. Military elite tensions with
Saleh over resource allocation. Southern elite increasingly alienated.
Oil revenue declining sharply. Active power struggles visible.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Houthi war. AQAP established. Multiple elite factions competing
openly. Ali Mohsen publicly distancing from Saleh. Business elite and
tribal sheikhs reconsidering alliances. Active power struggles within
ruling coalition.

2010.assessment:
Oil production falling sharply. Patronage system running low. Elite
defections increasing. Islah openly critical. Tribal leaders reducing
cooperation. Active power struggles as coalition fractures.

2011.assessment:
Complete fragmentation of ruling coalition. General Ali Mohsen defects
with 1st Armored Division March 2011. Islah, tribal sheikhs (Hashid),
business elite, diplomats all defect. Saleh injured June 2011 and
evacuated to Saudi Arabia. GCC deal negotiated among competing elites.
Complete fragmentation.

2012.assessment:
Hadi elected in February 2012 in single-candidate election. Transitional
coalition nominally unified but Saleh retaining GPC and some military
loyalties. Ali Mohsen and Islah aligned with Hadi. Multiple competing
elites within nominal government. Fragmentation persists.

2013.assessment:
National Dialogue Conference brings nominally inclusive elite participation
but underlying conflicts persist. Saleh-GPC sabotaging transition.
Houthis expanding militarily while attending NDC. Multiple competing
elite factions with different visions. Fragmentation.

2014.assessment:
Saleh-Houthi alliance forms (against Islah/Ali Mohsen). Houthis seize
Sanaa with tacit Saleh GPC/military cooperation. Hadi isolated. Complete
fragmentation with new axis forming. Elites divided into multiple camps
with no unified leadership.

2015.assessment:
Complete fragmentation into multiple armed factions: Houthis/Saleh (north),
Hadi/IRG (south), STC proto-forces (Aden), AQAP (south/east), ISIS elements.
Saudi coalition supporting Hadi from Riyadh. No unified elite. Multiple
competing governance projects.

2016.assessment:
Houthis/Saleh: uneasy partnership. Hadi government: Islah vs tribal factions.
STC forming under UAE auspices. Multiple competing authority structures.
Complete fragmentation with no single elite capable of national leadership.

2017.assessment:
Houthi-Saleh alliance collapses (Saleh killed December 2017). STC formally
established 2017 under UAE support. Hadi government fragmenting between
Islah and other factions. AQAP maintaining own elite structure. Multiple
competing elites with no possibility of unification.

2018.assessment:
Post-Saleh: Houthis sole authority in northwest. Hadi government vs STC
in south growing tension. Multiple military commanders competing. External
sponsors (Saudi, UAE, Iran) backing different factions. Complete fragmentation.

2019.assessment:
STC seizes Aden (August 2019), openly fighting Hadi government forces.
Three-way elite split: Houthis, IRG/Hadi, STC. Riyadh Agreement (November
2019) attempts reconciliation. Saudi-UAE sponsors now on different sides.
Maximum fragmentation.

2020.assessment:
Riyadh Agreement partially implemented but IRG-STC tensions persist.
Houthis internally cohesive but externally isolated. Multiple elite
structures competing. Saudi-UAE reconciliation attempt. Complete fragmentation.

2021.assessment:
Hadi government dysfunctional with PM Maeen Abdulmalik and VP Mohsen
competing. STC semi-autonomous in south. Houthis unified under Abdulmalik
al-Houthi. Complete fragmentation on anti-Houthi side. Houthis most
internally cohesive faction.

2022.assessment:
PLC formed April 2022 replacing Hadi (Saudi-engineered). PLC nominally
unifies IRG, STC, tribal factions. Some elite consolidation on government
side. Houthis remain unified but isolated. PLC is improvement over Hadi
dysfunction but still fragmented versus unified Houthi structure.

2023.assessment:
PLC managing coalition of factions with Saudi support. Houthis unified
but engaging in Saudi negotiations. Peace talks create some elite contact
across divide. PLC: active_power_struggles internally. Overall: complete
fragmentation at national level.

2024.assessment:
Houthis unified under Red Sea conflict leadership. PLC remains coalition
of factions with internal tensions. US/UK strikes on Houthis re-internationalizes
conflict. Elite fragmentation persists with no national unification possible.

2025.assessment:
Ongoing elite fragmentation. No unified national elite structure.
Houthis internally cohesive. PLC/STC coalition with internal divisions.
Peace talks stalled. No prospect of elite unification in near term.

1996.assessment:
Saleh's General People's Congress dominates. Parliament exists but rubber-stamps
executive. Judiciary nominally independent but subject to executive pressure.
Elections held but not fully competitive. Functioning but deeply flawed
authoritarian state institutions.

1997.assessment:
Parliamentary elections 1997 held with GPC dominant. Institutions
functioning within authoritarian framework. Opposition parties exist
but face constraints. Rule by tribal patron-client networks alongside
formal institutions.

1998.assessment:
Institutions functioning at basic level. Executive dominance continues.
Corruption pervasive. Security services primary instrument of governance.
Formal institutions supplemented by tribal patronage.

1999.assessment:
Presidential election held September 1999. Saleh wins with 96% in uncompetitive
vote. Institutions nominally functioning. Constitutional amendments
expand presidential powers. Authoritarian consolidation.

2000.assessment:
Security services dominant after USS Cole bombing. Legislature weak but
active. Judiciary influenced by executive. Growing security sector outside
formal institutional framework. Functioning authoritarian institutions.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 security dominates governance. Constitutional referendum expands
presidential term to 7 years. Institutions under executive control.
Counterterrorism cooperation with US complicates domestic governance.

2002.assessment:
Institutions functioning but degraded by security focus. Local council
elections held (some reform). Judiciary remains executive-controlled.
Patron-client networks increasingly displacing formal governance.

2003.assessment:
AQAP prison break embarrasses security apparatus. Institutions increasingly
strained by multiple security challenges. Parliament passing legitimating laws
but without real authority. Partial institutional function.

2004.assessment:
First Houthi war strains state capacity. Security apparatus deployed to
Saada. Military factionalism emerging. Civilian institutions continue
nominal functioning. Multi-front governance crisis beginning.

2005.assessment:
Second Houthi war. Parliamentary elections 2003 occurred under pressure.
Institutions degraded by multiple concurrent challenges. Service delivery
collapsing in peripheral areas. Security sector consuming state resources.

2006.assessment:
Presidential and local elections September 2006. Most competitive election
to date (Saleh wins with 77%). Third Houthi war. Some democratic functioning
but executive control maintained. Parliamentary activity continues.

2007.assessment:
Fourth Houthi war. Southern Movement growing. State capacity increasingly
stretched. Institutions partially functional but losing grip on peripheral
areas. Growing disjunction between formal institutions and governance reality.

2008.assessment:
Fifth Houthi war. US Embassy bombing. State capacity severely degraded.
Institutions nominal in peripheral areas. Government increasingly unable
to deliver services. Security sector dominant over civil institutions.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Houthi war. Saudi intervention. AQAP formalized. Parliament term
extended (postponed elections). Governance capacity at low ebb. Multiple
security fronts consuming all state attention. Severely degraded function.

2010.assessment:
Parliamentary elections postponed. AQAP parcel bomb plot. Southern Movement
protests. Governance legitimacy crisis. Institutions functioning only in
core areas. Deeply degraded state capacity.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests Jan-Nov 2011 paralyze governance. Military split
(Mohsen defection). AQAP seizes Zinjibar. GCC deal signed November 2011.
Saleh transfers power. Institutional collapse in most areas. Government
functioning only in core Sanaa administration.

2012.assessment:
Hadi inaugurated February 2012. National Dialogue Conference preparations.
Some institutional recovery. Government retakes Zinjibar. Parliament active.
Transitional institutions functioning at minimal level. Partial recovery.

2013.assessment:
National Dialogue Conference ongoing. Transitional institutions partially
functioning. Houthi expansion continuing. AQAP active. Security sector
fragmented. Minimal institutional function at national level.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa September 2014. Place Hadi under house arrest
January 2015. Parliament effectively suspended. Ministerial functions
captured by Houthi Revolutionary Committee. Full institutional collapse
of recognized government.

2015.assessment:
Full institutional collapse. Hadi government in exile in Riyadh. Houthis
operate parallel Revolutionary Committee governance in Sanaa. IRG/Hadi
nominal institutions in south with coalition backing. No legitimate
functioning national institutions.

2016.assessment:
Continued dual-governance structure. Houthi Supreme Political Council
(August 2016) formalizes parallel governance with Saleh GPC. Hadi
government in Aden/Riyadh. Neither functions as national government.

2017.assessment:
Continued parallel governance systems. Houthi Revolutionary Committee
administers Sanaa and northwest. Hadi government in Aden. CBY split
(Houthi CBY in Sanaa vs CBY-Aden). Saleh killed December 2017.

2018.assessment:
Three competing governance authorities (Houthis, IRG, STC). No national
parliament meets. Banking system fractured. Complete absence of functional
national institutions.

2019.assessment:
STC seizes Aden August 2019. Three-way fragmentation complete.
Riyadh Agreement November 2019 attempts reconciliation with minimal
implementation. National institutions absent or competing.

2020.assessment:
Continued fragmentation. COVID-19 overwhelms nominal health institutions.
No national budget functions. Three competing administrative systems.
No functional national government.

2021.assessment:
Fragmentation continues. Hadi government and Houthi de-facto authority
each control parallel systems. STC with its own administration in south.
No national Parliament. Multiple local administrative systems competing.

2022.assessment:
Presidential Leadership Council (PLC) replaces Hadi April 2022. PLC
nominally unites IRG and STC factions. Some consolidation on government
side but Houthi parallel state persists. Still collapsed at national level.

2023.assessment:
PLC functioning as nominal authority in government-held areas. Houthi
de-facto government in northwest. Peace negotiations ongoing. Two parallel
governance systems. Collapsed at national level.

2024.assessment:
PLC nominal authority in south/east. Houthi de-facto authority in northwest.
US/UK strikes on Houthis add new dimension. No national institutions function.

2025.assessment:
Ongoing institutional fragmentation. No national government functions.
PLC controls south/east, Houthis control northwest. No elections held
since 2012. Peace process stalled.

1996.assessment:
Relatively low-level violence. Tribal conflicts and sporadic government
clashes. No major armed conflict but persistent low-intensity violence
from tribal feuds and criminal activity. Some southern discontent.

1997.assessment:
Low-level violence. Parliamentary elections held peacefully. Tribal clashes
and periodic kidnappings. Al-Iman network growing but not yet operational
as major force. State repression limited.

1998.assessment:
Kidnappings and tribal violence increasing. Islamist extremist networks
active. Aden-Abyan Islamic Army incident (kidnapping of Western tourists,
4 killed December 1998). Low but rising violence trajectory.

1999.assessment:
Continued low-level violence. Presidential election held. Islamist extremists
active. Tribal conflicts ongoing. Government manages security through tribal
patronage networks rather than rule of law.

2000.assessment:
USS Cole bombing October 2000 (17 US sailors killed). Al-Qaeda operational.
Tribal violence continuing. Government forces clash with tribal militias.
Rising jihadist violence trajectory.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 crackdown on al-Qaeda in Yemen. Government-jihadist clashes.
Tribal violence and kidnappings continue. US-Yemen counterterrorism
cooperation intensifies. Moderate violence level overall.

2002.assessment:
Limburg tanker attack October 2002. US drone strike kills Qaed Salim Sinan
al-Harethi (AQAP leader) November 2002. Increasing counterterrorism operations
against AQAP. Moderate violence with rising trajectory.

2003.assessment:
Al-Qaeda prison break April 2003 (10 operatives including USS Cole planner).
AQAP reconstituting. Government fights both tribal actors and jihadists.
Southern discontent producing protests and clashes.

2004.assessment:
First Houthi war erupts June 2004 in Saada. Heavy fighting between
government forces and Houthi movement. Hussein al-Houthi killed September 2004.
Significant casualties on both sides. First major internal armed conflict
of post-unification Yemen.

2005.assessment:
Second Houthi war. Continued fighting in Saada. Tribal clashes and AQAP
activity ongoing. Multiple fronts of violence straining government. Southern
movement protests sometimes violent.

2006.assessment:
Third Houthi war. AQAP plot disrupted. Southern Movement forming.
Political violence from multiple actors. Government manages but does not
resolve conflicts.

2007.assessment:
Fourth Houthi war. Heavy fighting in Saada. AQAP attacks increasing
in south and east. Tourist kidnappings. Southern Movement protests
suppressed. Multiple active conflict streams.

2008.assessment:
Fifth Houthi war. US Embassy bombing September 2008 (16 dead). AQAP
attacks on tourists (March 2008). Heavy fighting in Saada ongoing.
Violence on multiple fronts becoming normalized.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Houthi war August 2009. Saudi Arabia launches Operation Scorched Earth
against Houthis. AQAP formally established. Largest conflict death toll
since unification. Southern Movement protests violent suppression.

2010.assessment:
Relative decrease after sixth Houthi war ceasefire but AQAP attacks
increasing. Cargo plane bomb plot (October 2010). US drone strikes
intensifying. Southern Movement protests suppressed. Moderate-high violence.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests suppressed violently. Saleh injured in attack on
presidential compound (June 2011). Government forces and tribal militias
clash. AQAP seizes Zinjibar. Assassination attempt on Saleh. Multiple
armed actors. High violence year.

2012.assessment:
Transition period. Government forces retake Zinjibar from AQAP. Drone
strikes intensify against AQAP. Houthi expansion beginning. Transitional
government establishing authority. Violence declining from 2011 peak.

2013.assessment:
AQAP drone strikes very frequent. Houthis advancing from Saada.
National Dialogue Conference ongoing amid continuing violence. Army
factionalism. Multiple armed actors. Violence rising again.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa September 2014. Rapid territorial advances with heavy
fighting. Houthi-Saleh alliance vs government forces. AQAP attacks continuing.
Multiple fronts of violence. Rapidly deteriorating security.

2015.assessment:
Saudi-led coalition intervention March 2015 massively escalates conflict.
Airstrikes, ground combat, naval blockade. Aden battle (March-July 2015).
Coalition bombing campaigns across Yemen. Casualty estimates vary widely
but tens of thousands killed. Catastrophic escalation.

2016.assessment:
Continued high-intensity conflict. Coalition airstrikes sustained. Ground
fighting largely static on multiple fronts. Cholera epidemic beginning.
UN peace talks (Kuwait) collapse. Very high violence levels.

2017.assessment:
Continued high-intensity conflict. Cholera epidemic (largest in modern history).
Coalition blockade tightens after Houthi missile at Riyadh (November 2017).
Saleh killed by Houthis December 2017. Still very high violence.

2018.assessment:
Battle for Hodeidah June-December 2018 (major offensive). Stockholm Agreement
December 2018 halts Hodeidah offensive. Heavy fighting around Hodeidah port
threatens humanitarian lifeline. Very high annual casualty toll.

2019.assessment:
Somewhat reduced fighting post-Stockholm Agreement though ceasefire violated
repeatedly. STC seizes Aden from IRG August 2019. Houthi drone attacks on
Saudi Aramco September 2019. Still severe violence levels.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 reduces some operations. Houthis advance in Marib governorate.
Continued coalition airstrikes. IRG-STC clashes in south. Still high
violence but somewhat below 2015-2018 peak. UN ceasefire calls ignored.

2021.assessment:
Intensified Houthi offensive on Marib. Heavy fighting around Marib city.
Coalition airstrikes resume. Houthis attack UAE with drones and ballistic
missiles. Highest ground combat intensity since 2018. Sharp violence increase.

2022.assessment:
UN-brokered truce April-October 2022 dramatically reduces violence. Houthi
Marib offensive stalls. Coalition strikes reduced during truce. Significant
violence reduction compared to 2021 peak. Post-truce expiry partial calm holds.

2023.assessment:
Fragile ceasefire broadly holds. Saudi-Iran rapprochement reduces coalition
pressure on Houthis. Houthi-Saudi negotiations ongoing. Significant reduction
from peak violence. Localized clashes continue along frontlines.

2024.assessment:
Houthis begin Red Sea shipping attacks (from October/November 2023).
US/UK launch airstrikes on Yemen from January 2024. New internationalized
dimension but land frontlines static. Violence increases from 2023 but
below 2015-2021 peak levels.

2025.assessment:
Ongoing conflict. US/UK strikes on Houthis continuing. Land frontlines
broadly static. Houthi Red Sea attacks ongoing. Moderate-high violence
compared to pre-2015 but well below 2015-2021 peak.

1996.assessment:
Saleh government controls most of unified Yemen (unified 1990). Minor tribal
insurgencies and periodic unrest in south but central government authority
largely intact. Border disputes with Saudi Arabia settled 2000.

1997.assessment:
Saleh government maintains firm control over unified Yemen. Parliamentary
elections held in 1997. Tribal authority supplementing but not replacing
central government. Southern discontent simmering but controlled.

1998.assessment:
Government control stable. Islamist militants (later al-Qaeda affiliated)
beginning to operate in peripheral areas. Kidnappings of foreigners signal
diminishing authority in tribal areas. Aden bombing threats.

1999.assessment:
Saleh re-elected. Government control largely intact but Islamist extremist
network growing in peripheral areas. Tribal areas operate semi-autonomously
with informal government approval.

2000.assessment:
USS Cole bombing in Aden October 2000 highlights security gaps. Saudi-Yemen
border demarcation agreement signed. Government control generally intact
but tribal periphery increasingly ungoverned. Al-Qaeda presence growing.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 crackdown on al-Qaeda. Yemen cooperates with US counterterrorism.
Government control challenged by al-Qaeda networks in eastern provinces.
Tribal areas remain outside effective government reach.

2002.assessment:
Limburg tanker attack October 2002. AQAP consolidating in Marib, Abyan,
and Hadramawt. US drone strikes begin. Government authority nominal in
several eastern provinces.

2003.assessment:
Government control declining in periphery. Al-Qaeda prison break in Sanaa.
Eastern tribal areas largely ungoverned. Southern discontent growing.
US counterterrorism cooperation continues.

2004.assessment:
First Houthi war erupts in Saada governorate (June 2004). Hussein al-Houthi
killed September 2004. Northern Saada province becomes active conflict zone.
Government battles simultaneously with AQAP in south/east and Houthis in north.

2005.assessment:
Second Houthi war. Saada governorate remains contested. AQAP active in
eastern and southern provinces. Central government increasingly stretched
between multiple security challenges.

2006.assessment:
Third Houthi war. Northern Saada persistently contested. Saleh re-elected
in competitive elections. Southern Movement (Hirak) beginning to organize.
AQAP network expanding.

2007.assessment:
Fourth Houthi war. Saada northern governorate largely outside government control.
Southern separatist movement growing. AQAP attacking security forces.
Government increasingly unable to project authority beyond urban centers.

2008.assessment:
Fifth Houthi war. US Embassy attack in Sanaa (September 2008). Multiple
fronts of conflict straining government capacity. AQAP bombers active.
Northern and eastern governorates increasingly beyond central reach.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Houthi war (August 2009). Saudi Arabia intervenes against Houthis.
AQAP formally established. Saada and parts of north outside government control.
Government authority increasingly fragmented across multiple conflict zones.

2010.assessment:
Relative calm after sixth Houthi war ceasefire. AQAP very active (parcel
bomb plot). Southern Movement protests large. Government nominal authority
in several areas. Multi-layered governance crisis deepening.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests Jan-Nov 2011. Saleh signs GCC transition deal November 2011.
AQAP seizes Zinjibar (Abyan capital) and other southern territory. Defection
of General Ali Mohsen al-Ahmar with forces. Houthis control Saada. Government
authority effectively collapsed in much of north and south.

2012.assessment:
Hadi becomes transitional president. Government forces retake Zinjibar from AQAP.
National Dialogue Conference begins preparations. Houthis still control Saada.
Southern separatist movement active but not controlling territory. Slight improvement.

2013.assessment:
National Dialogue Conference ongoing. Hadi government with limited authority.
Houthis expanding from Saada southward. AQAP retaining presence in south/east.
Tribal areas nominally under government but not effectively administered.

2014.assessment:
Houthis seize Sanaa in September 2014. Amran falls July. Houthis take
Hodeidah. Massive territorial expansion southward. Hadi under house arrest.
Effective government control collapses to southern provinces and parts of center.

2015.assessment:
Hadi flees to Riyadh January 2015. Saudi-led coalition intervenes March 2015.
Houthis and Saleh forces control Sanaa, northwest, Hodeidah. IRG/Hadi control
south and east with coalition support. STC proto-authority in Aden. Catastrophic
fragmentation with no single authority controlling majority of population.

2016.assessment:
Conflict stalemated. Houthis control Sanaa and northwest (majority population).
IRG forces regain Aden but STC (Southern Transitional Council) competing for
authority. AQAP controls Mukalla. Frontlines roughly stable. No legitimate
single authority.

2017.assessment:
Persistent fragmentation. Houthis control northwest/Sanaa (about 70-80% of
population). IRG with coalition controls south/east. STC forces in Aden
competing with IRG. Frontlines largely static in 2017.

2018.assessment:
Saleh killed December 2017 (Houthis). Battle for Hodeidah begins June 2018.
Stockholm Agreement December 2018 halts Hodeidah offensive. STC increasingly
independent from Hadi government. Multiple competing authorities persist.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on Yemen

2019.assessment:
STC declares self-governance in Aden August 2019, expels Hadi government.
Three-way split: Houthis (northwest), IRG (center/east), STC (south).
Riyadh Agreement November 2019 attempts IRG-STC reconciliation with limited
success.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 adds to humanitarian catastrophe. Houthis advance in Marib.
Power-sharing agreement between IRG and STC partially implemented.
Houthi attacks on Saudi Arabia intensify. Fragmentation persists.
No single authority controls more than ~25% of territory.

2021.assessment:
Houthis intensify Marib offensive. Presidential Leadership Council not yet
formed. Houthis strengthening control over northwest. IRG/STC coalition
fragmented. Houthis control majority of Yemen's populated areas.

2022.assessment:
UN-brokered truce April 2022 holds through October. Presidential Leadership
Council (PLC) replaces Hadi April 2022. Houthi Marib offensive stalled.
Fragmentation remains: Houthis control northwest (majority population),
PLC/STC control south and east. Truce brings partial calm.

2023.assessment:
Fragile ceasefire continues post-truce. Saudi-Iran rapprochement (March 2023)
raises hopes for Yemen peace. Houthis and Saudi Arabia engage in back-channel
talks. Territorial lines unchanged: Houthis northwest, PLC/STC south-east.
No decisive outcome.

2024.assessment:
Houthis begin Red Sea shipping attacks (November 2023 onward) prompting
US/UK airstrikes on Yemen January 2024. No major territorial changes.
Houthis control northwest; PLC/STC control south. Fragile ceasefire on
land holds despite new internationalized conflict dimension.

2025.assessment:
Continued fragmentation. Houthis control northwest. PLC/STC control south
and east. US airstrikes on Houthis ongoing. Peace negotiations stalled.
No resolution expected in near term. Humanitarian crisis persists.

1996.assessment: UNDP HDI value of 0.405 — low human development.
