1996.assessment:
No IBP coverage for Yemen before 2006. Authoritarian state with limited
budget transparency. Government budgets published but limited detail.
Oil revenues poorly disclosed. Minimal budget transparency expected.

1997.assessment:
Limited budget transparency. Government budget documents available but
not comprehensive. Oil revenue disclosure minimal. Scant-to-minimal.

1998.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Oil revenues not fully disclosed.
Security spending opaque. Scant transparency.

1999.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Authoritarian governance limiting
public access to fiscal information.

2000.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Oil revenues from Hunt Oil and TotalEnergies
not publicly disclosed. Budget documents limited in detail.

2001.assessment:
Limited budget transparency. Security spending classified.
Oil revenues opaque. Scant public information.

2002.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Government publishes summary budget figures
but detailed disclosure lacking.

2003.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Authoritarian state limiting public
oversight. Scant information.

2004.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Houthi war spending classified.
Budget oversight limited.

2005.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Oil revenues, security spending poorly disclosed.
Scant public information.

2006.assessment:
IBP Open Budget Survey 2006 covers Yemen with score of approximately 10/100.
Scant budget transparency. Government providing minimal public budget
information. Oil revenues not adequately disclosed.

2007.assessment:
IBP OBI score approximately 10 (2008 survey covers 2007). Scant budget
transparency. Government providing minimal information. Among lowest globally.

2008.assessment:
IBP OBI Yemen approximately 10/100. Scant budget transparency.
Security spending classified. Oil revenues opaque.

2009.assessment:
IBP OBI score approximately 11. Scant budget transparency.
No significant improvement from previous years.

2010.assessment:
IBP OBI approximately 11. Scant budget transparency. Parliamentary
elections postponed. Budget oversight non-functional.

2011.assessment:
IBP OBI score approximately 8. Arab Spring and political crisis
reduce already-minimal budget transparency. Emergency spending
without disclosure. Scant to no information.

2012.assessment:
IBP OBI approximately 8. Transitional government with minimal budget
transparency. International aid not well tracked. Scant transparency.

2013.assessment:
IBP OBI approximately 8. Minimal budget transparency. Security spending
classified. Oil revenues not fully disclosed. Scant.

2014.assessment:
IBP OBI declining. Houthis seize Sanaa disrupting government budget
process. Fiscal information increasingly unavailable. Approaching
no_information level.

2015.assessment:
IBP stops covering Yemen after 2015 due to conflict. Dual fiscal
authorities (Houthi CBY-Sanaa vs government CBY-Aden). No budget
information available. Complete collapse of budget transparency.

2016.assessment:
No budget information available. Dual fiscal authorities. Civil war.
Complete absence of budget transparency.

2017.assessment:
No budget transparency. Civil war. Two parallel budget systems
(Houthi and government). No public information available.

2018.assessment:
No budget transparency. Civil war. Two competing fiscal authorities.
No information.

2019.assessment:
No budget transparency. Two competing fiscal systems. STC adds third
fiscal actor. No budget information.

2020.assessment:
No budget transparency. Civil war. Multiple fiscal authorities.
No information available to public.

2021.assessment:
No budget transparency. Fiscal collapse with competing systems.
No information.

2022.assessment:
UN truce does not restore budget transparency. PLC government attempts
some fiscal management but no public disclosure. No information from
either fiscal authority.

2023.assessment:
No budget transparency. Competing fiscal systems. Peace process
does not address fiscal transparency. No information.

2024.assessment:
No budget transparency. Ongoing conflict. Multiple competing fiscal
systems. No public budget information from any authority.

1996.assessment:
Yemen not an EITI member. Oil revenues flow through state oil company
(YOGC/later MOM) with minimal public disclosure. Oil production ~400,000
bpd; revenues dominate budget but detailed breakdowns not published.

sources.citation: US Energy Information Administration. Yemen Country Profile. 2000.
World Bank. Yemen Economic Monitoring Report 1997.

1997.assessment:
No EITI membership. Oil revenue reporting through government budget
documents but limited detail on production sharing agreements. International
oil companies (Hunt Oil, Total) operate without public revenue disclosure.

sources.citation: US Energy Information Administration. Yemen Oil Data 1997.
Central Bank of Yemen. Annual Report 1997.

1998.assessment:
Oil prices crash reduces revenues but disclosure practices unchanged.
No EITI. Oil sector opaque; revenue from Masila and Marib blocks managed
without public accountability.

1999.assessment:
Oil revenues recovering with price rebound but extractive transparency
unchanged. No EITI. Production sharing agreements with international oil
companies remain confidential.

sources.citation: US Energy Information Administration. Yemen Country Profile. 2000.

2000.assessment:
High oil prices generate significant revenues with no public disclosure
mechanism. EITI Initiative not yet launched globally (launched 2002).
Yemen's oil revenue management opaque at ministerial level.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance of Yemen. Budget Documents 2000.

2001.assessment:
Oil production at peak (~440,000 bpd in 2001). No extractive transparency
mechanisms. Revenue flows opaque; corruption in oil sector significant per
Transparency International.

sources.citation: Transparency International. Corruption Perceptions Index 2001. Yemen.

2002.assessment:
EITI Initiative launched globally at World Summit on Sustainable Development
(Johannesburg, 2002). Yemen not among early adopters. Oil revenue management
continues without public disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. History of EITI. https://eiti.org/history

2003.assessment:
Yemen shows no interest in EITI candidacy despite declining oil reserves
and growing fiscal pressure. Oil production declining (~400,000 bpd).
Revenue transparency not a government priority.

2004.assessment:
No EITI engagement. Oil production declining (~370,000 bpd). Saleh
government recognizes oil dependency but does not adopt transparency
measures. Revenue flows continue through opaque government channels.

sources.citation: Ministry of Oil and Minerals of Yemen. Annual Report 2004.

2005.assessment:
EITI Board created in 2005. Yemen still not a candidate. Oil production
~350,000 bpd and declining. Donor pressure for revenue transparency not
yet effective.

2006.assessment:
Yemen begins EITI candidacy process in 2006-2007 period. EITI standard
requires disclosure of payments by companies and receipts by government.
Candidacy marks first formal extractive transparency commitment.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen EITI Candidacy. https://eiti.org/yemen

2007.assessment:
Yemen formally admitted as EITI Candidate country. EITI Multi-Stakeholder
Group (MSG) established with government, civil society, and industry
representatives. First EITI report being prepared. Significant step toward
extractive transparency.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen EITI Candidacy Update 2007.

2008.assessment:
Yemen EITI candidate status. First EITI reconciliation report published
covering fiscal year 2006. Report reconciles oil company payments with
government receipts. MSG active. Process builds extractive transparency
framework.

sources.citation: Yemen EITI. First EITI Reconciliation Report (FY2006). 2008.

2009.assessment:
EITI candidate status maintained. Second EITI report covering FY2007 in
preparation. Sa'ada conflict and deteriorating security affect MSG activities
but process continues. Oil production declining to ~300,000 bpd.

sources.citation: Yemen EITI. Second EITI Reconciliation Report (FY2007). 2009.

2010.assessment:
Yemen declared EITI Compliant in 2010, first country in Middle East to
achieve EITI Compliant status. Oil and LNG revenues disclosed; reconciliation
reports published annually. Validation completed successfully.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen Achieves EITI Compliant Status. Press release, 2010.

2011.assessment:
Yemen EITI Compliant status (achieved 2010). Despite Arab Spring turmoil,
EITI process continues. MSG meets; EITI reports published for FY2009-2010.
Extractive transparency maintained at institutional level even as political
stability collapses.

sources.citation: Yemen EITI. EITI Report FY2009. Published 2011.

2012.assessment:
EITI Compliant status maintained under Hadi transition government. EITI
revalidation process ongoing. EITI reports cover oil, gas, and mineral
sectors. Transition government views EITI compliance as governance credibility
signal to donors.

sources.citation: Yemen EITI. EITI Report FY2010-2011. Published 2012.

2013.assessment:
EITI Compliant status maintained. New EITI Standard adopted globally in
2013 (more demanding). Yemen working toward compliance with expanded standard
covering beneficial ownership and sub-national transfers.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. EITI Standard 2013. New requirements.

2014.assessment:
Houthi takeover disrupts EITI process. MSG meetings increasingly difficult
to convene. Hadi government still nominally EITI compliant but operational
capacity declining. EITI Secretariat monitoring situation. Oil production
declining sharply.

2015.assessment:
EITI Board suspends Yemen from EITI in June 2015 due to armed conflict
preventing implementation. Civil war prevents MSG from meeting, collecting
data, or publishing reports. Oil infrastructure damaged; Aden refinery shut
down. Extractive transparency collapses with civil war.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen Suspension Decision, June 2015. https://eiti.org/news/eiti-board-suspends-yemen
Natural Resource Governance Institute. Yemen Oil Sector in Conflict. 2015.

2016.assessment:
Yemen remains EITI suspended. Civil war disrupts all oil sector operations
and reporting. No reconciliation reports possible. Marib oil fields change
hands multiple times. Revenue flows to competing authorities with no
transparency.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen Country Page 2016. https://eiti.org/yemen
UN Panel of Experts on Yemen. Final Report S/2017/81. 2017.

2017.assessment:
EITI suspension continues. IRG controls some oil-producing areas (Marib,
Shabwa) and exports crude via Aden. Houthi forces periodically attack Marib
oil infrastructure. No formal extractive transparency; revenues support
military operations without oversight.

sources.citation: Natural Resource Governance Institute. Yemen Oil Revenues During Conflict. 2018.

2018.assessment:
Yemen EITI remains suspended. Stockholm Agreement ceasefire negotiations
but no EITI reinstatement. IRG oil exports continue without EITI oversight.
Dual oil revenue streams (IRG vs. Houthi-taxed oil transit) with no public
accounting.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Yemen Suspended Country Update 2018.

2019.assessment:
EITI suspension continues. Hodeidah ceasefire under Stockholm Agreement
reduces fighting near port but extractive transparency not restored. Oil
production minimal. EITI reinstatement would require MSG reconvening and
multi-stakeholder reporting - impossible under conflict conditions.

2020.assessment:
Yemen remains EITI suspended. Oil exports halted for extended periods due
to conflict and infrastructure damage. No MSG activity possible. COVID-19
adds additional barrier to any reinstatement process. Oil revenues from
Marib block disputed between IRG and local forces.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on Yemen. Final Report S/2021/79. 2021.

2021.assessment:
EITI suspension continues into sixth year. No prospects for reinstatement
while civil war ongoing. Houthis contest Marib city approaches; battle for
Marib oil fields ongoing. STC-controlled areas in south have separate oil
revenue streams. Total opacity in extractive sector.

2022.assessment:
UN-brokered truce (April 2022) holds for several months but EITI not
reinstated. PLC formation creates political structure but EITI requires
MSG with civil society, government, and industry - not possible under
conflict. Oil exports resume in IRG areas but without EITI oversight.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Yemen's Fragile Truce 2022.

2023.assessment:
Saudi-Iran normalization deal opens diplomatic space but no EITI
reinstatement discussions reported. Oil revenues from Marib fields managed
by IRG/PLC without transparency framework. Extractive sector remains opaque.

2024.assessment:
Yemen remains EITI suspended. Houthi Red Sea attacks prompt US/UK airstrikes;
conflict escalates. EITI reinstatement impossible. Oil revenue discussions
between IRG and Saudi Arabia without public disclosure. Ninth year of EITI
suspension.

sources.citation: Natural Resource Governance Institute. MENA Extractive Sector Update 2024.

2025.assessment:
Yemen projected to remain EITI suspended. No peace agreement reached.
Any EITI reinstatement would require political settlement, MSG reconvening,
and multi-stakeholder reporting process - all dependent on peace. Extractive
transparency remains absent.

sources.citation: Dresch, Paul. A History of Modern Yemen. Cambridge University Press, 2000.
Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1996. Country report Yemen.

1997.assessment:
Legal transparency remains limited. Judicial independence weak; commercial
law poorly enforced. Laws published sporadically in official gazette but
access limited for most citizens.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1997. Country report Yemen.

1998.assessment:
No significant improvements in legal transparency. Judicial system remains
underfunded and subject to political interference. Regulations applied
inconsistently across regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1998. Country report Yemen.

1999.assessment:
Legal framework continues to lack transparency. Customary and tribal law
often supersedes formal legal channels in rural areas. Official legal texts
not systematically accessible.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1999. Country report Yemen.

2000.assessment:
Some administrative reforms attempted but legal transparency unchanged.
Judiciary not independent; executive decrees remain primary governance
mechanism in many areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2000. Country report Yemen.
World Bank. Yemen: Governance and Public Sector Reform. 2001.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 counterterrorism cooperation brings some external scrutiny of
legal systems but domestic legal transparency unchanged. Courts remain
politicized.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2002: Yemen.

2002.assessment:
Legal framework continues to exhibit significant gaps. Business regulations
unpredictable; rule of law weak. Government relies heavily on presidential
decrees for major decisions.

sources.citation: World Bank. Doing Business in Yemen. 2003.
Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2002. Country report Yemen.

2003.assessment:
No substantial improvement in legal transparency. Judiciary still subject
to executive influence; laws remain inaccessible to most citizens.
Corruption undermines rule of law.

sources.citation: Transparency International. Corruption Perceptions Index 2003. Yemen.

2004.assessment:
Sa'ada conflict beginning creates additional legal vacuum in northern
regions. Central legal institutions remain weak and poorly accessible.
Regulations enforced selectively.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2005: Yemen.

2005.assessment:
Legal transparency remains poor. Oil revenue windfalls directed through
non-transparent mechanisms. Sa'ada conflict expands legal vacuum in
northwest.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2006: Yemen.

2006.assessment:
Presidential elections held but legal framework remains opaque. Judiciary
formally independent but practically subordinate to executive. WJP data
not yet available for Yemen.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2006. Country report Yemen.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2008: Yemen.

2008.assessment:
Legal transparency marginally improves with some reform efforts but
structural problems persist. Courts backlogged; laws not systematically
published or accessible.

sources.citation: World Bank. Doing Business 2008. Yemen profile.

2009.assessment:
Sixth Sa'ada war creates large areas of legal inaccessibility. Overall
rule of law deteriorates. Emergency provisions expand executive
decree-making.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2010: Yemen.

2010.assessment:
AQAP expansion in southern provinces further erodes legal framework.
State authority and legal institutions increasingly contested. Regime
survival displaces rule of law priorities.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Yemen: Defusing the Saada Time Bomb. 2009.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring uprising causes near-total breakdown of formal legal institutions.
Courts suspend operations in many areas; state authority collapses in southern
provinces and Sa'ada. GCC deal provides framework but legal institutions remain
non-functional.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2012: Yemen.
Phillips, Sarah. Yemen and the Politics of Permanent Crisis. IISS/Routledge, 2011.

2012.assessment:
Transition government under Hadi attempts some legal reforms. National
Dialogue Conference preparation creates some openness. But enforcement
capacity extremely limited; AQAP controls significant territory with no
formal law.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2012. Country report Yemen.

2013.assessment:
National Dialogue Conference ongoing; draft constitution being developed.
Legal framework debates underway but formal legal transparency remains poor.
NDC creates some accessible legal discourse.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2014: Yemen.
National Yemen. National Dialogue Conference final documents, 2013.

2014.assessment:
Houthi takeover of Sanaa in September 2014 begins breakdown of legal
order. NDC outcomes not implemented. Multiple competing legal systems
emerging (formal government, Houthi, AQAP). WJP not covering Yemen.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. The Huthis: From Saada to Sanaa. ICG Middle East Report N154, June 2014.

2015.assessment:
Civil war produces complete fragmentation of legal frameworks. Houthi areas
governed by revolutionary committee decrees without legal basis. Saudi-backed
government issues laws from Aden/Riyadh with no enforcement capacity. AQAP
establishes sharia courts in controlled areas. No accessible unified legal
framework.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2016: Yemen.
Amnesty International. Yemen: All sides committing war crimes. 2015.

2016.assessment:
No unified legal framework. Houthi Supreme Political Council issues arbitrary
decrees; internationally recognized government publishes laws from exile with
no enforcement. Multiple competing legal systems operate across fragmented
territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2016. Country report Yemen.
UN Panel of Experts on Yemen. Final Report S/2017/81. January 2017.

2017.assessment:
Legal system completely dysfunctional at national level. No accessible
official legal framework for most citizens. Houthi areas under decree rule;
IRG areas nominally subject to pre-war laws but enforcement absent. Courts
operate sporadically in some areas.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2018: Yemen.

2018.assessment:
Legal transparency remains absent across Yemen. Stockholm Agreement
negotiations underway but no legal framework reform. Houthi revolutionary
courts sentence opponents without legal due process. IRG legal institutions
lack capacity and territory.

sources.citation: UN Yemen Experts Panel. S/2019/83. January 2019.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2019: Yemen.

2019.assessment:
STC declares self-administration in south (2020), further fragmenting legal
landscape. Houthi areas continue arbitrary decree rule. IRG courts in Aden
have some limited function but highly constrained.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Yemen: An Elusive Peace. 2019.

2020.assessment:
Riyadh Agreement between IRG and STC nominally restores unity government
but separate legal systems persist. COVID-19 eliminates any residual court
function. No accessible legal framework for ordinary citizens. Houthi courts
continue sentencing journalists and activists to death.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on Yemen. Final Report S/2021/79. January 2021.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2021: Yemen.

2021.assessment:
Presidential Leadership Council (PLC) not yet formed; IRG government remains
fragmented. No functioning national legal framework. Houthi areas under
quasi-theocratic decree law. Southern areas under STC parallel administration.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2021. Country report Yemen.

2022.assessment:
Presidential Leadership Council formed April 2022; UN-brokered truce reduces
violence. Some symbolic steps toward legal normalization in IRG-controlled
areas but no substantive legal transparency improvements. Houthi areas
continue operating outside any recognized legal framework.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Yemen's Fragile Truce. 2022.
UN Yemen Experts Panel. Final Report S/2023/66. January 2023.

2023.assessment:
Saudi-Iran deal enables some IRG-Houthi diplomatic contact but legal
frameworks remain entirely separate and non-transparent. No WJP data for
Yemen. Multiple competing legal authorities continue.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. World Report 2024: Yemen.

2024.assessment:
Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping prompt US/UK airstrikes. Houthi-controlled
areas remain under arbitrary decree law; IRG areas still lack enforcement
capacity. No accessible unified legal framework. SDGT designation of Houthis
adds economic pressure but does not restore legal order.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Yemen: The Houthi Maritime Campaign. 2024.

2025.assessment:
No peace settlement reached. Legal framework remains fragmented across
Houthi, IRG/PLC, STC-administered areas. No accessible national legal
framework. Ceasefire negotiations ongoing but without legal reform component.

sources.citation: UN OSESGY (UN Special Envoy for Yemen). Briefings to Security Council, 2025.

1996.assessment:
No RSF data pre-2002. Based on Freedom House qualitative assessments.
Yemen has some independent press but severe government restrictions.
Journalists harassed, jailed. Very serious press freedom situation.

1997.assessment:
Very serious press freedom situation. Government controls major media.
Independent newspapers exist but face censorship and harassment.
Very serious environment.

1998.assessment:
Very serious press freedom environment. Government media dominant.
Independent press marginally allowed but faces suppression.
Journalists imprisoned. Very serious.

1999.assessment:
Very serious press freedom situation. Government uses broad anti-press
laws to silence criticism. Very serious environment.

2000.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. USS Cole bombing increases government
sensitivity to critical coverage. Journalists arrested.

2001.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Post-9/11 counterterrorism framing used
to jail journalists covering AQAP with any nuance.

2002.assessment:
RSF begins tracking Yemen. Score approximately 65 (RSF scale inverted:
higher RSF score = worse). Difficult to very serious press environment.
Journalists jailed for covering sensitive topics.

2003.assessment:
Very serious press freedom situation. Government using national security
laws to silence reporters. Independent newspapers operating under self-censorship.

2004.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. First Houthi war reporting restricted.
Journalists covering Saada conflict arrested or denied access.

2005.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Multiple Houthi wars restrict northern
coverage. Southern Movement reporting restricted. Journalists imprisoned.

2006.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Election year brings some opening but
government restricts coverage of Houthi wars and opposition. Very serious.

2007.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Journalists covering Houthi conflicts
and Southern Movement arrested. Self-censorship pervasive.

2008.assessment:
Very serious press freedom worsening. Fifth Houthi war. US Embassy
bombing. Multiple journalists jailed. Press freedom deteriorating.

2009.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Sixth Houthi war. AQAP activities.
Journalists covering both fronts face severe restrictions and imprisonment.

2010.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. AQAP operational security prevents
coverage. Government restricts Southern Movement coverage. Very serious.

2011.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Arab Spring protests. Government attacks
on journalists covering protests (including killings). Media freedom
severely repressed. Very serious.

2012.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Some improvement during transition period
but baseline remains very serious. Journalists released from prison.
Still very serious environment.

2013.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. National Dialogue Conference media coverage
allowed. But Houthi intimidation of journalists in north. AQAP threats.
Very serious overall.

2014.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Houthis seize Sanaa and take over state
media. Kidnap journalists. Media in Houthi areas now fully controlled.
Deteriorating sharply.

2015.assessment:
Total press freedom collapse. Civil war makes journalism life-threatening.
Houthi media control in north. Coalition bombing of media facilities.
Multiple journalists killed. No_press_freedom to very_serious in all territories.

2016.assessment:
No press freedom. Houthis control all media in northwest. Journalists
in government areas face coalition-related restrictions. Multiple journalists
detained by Houthis. Very serious to no_press_freedom.

2017.assessment:
Very serious bordering no_press_freedom. Houthi media control complete
in northwest. South: UAE-backed forces detaining journalists. Multiple
journalists killed. Yemen among worst globally.

2018.assessment:
Very serious. Houthi media suppression in north. Multiple journalists
killed or held hostage by Houthis. Coalition airstrikes on media.
No independent journalism possible in conflict zones.

2019.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Houthis holding multiple journalists
since 2015 including RSF journalist Yahya al-Jubaihi. Both sides
suppress independent coverage.

2020.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Houthis sentence journalists to death
(four journalists sentenced to death for 'espionage' 2020). Among
worst globally for press freedom.

2021.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Journalists held by Houthis since 2015 still
imprisoned. Four journalists on death row. No independent journalism
in conflict zones.

2022.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. UN truce does not improve press freedom.
Long-held journalists remain imprisoned. Four journalists still on
death row. Very serious environment.

2023.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Some journalists released in prisoner
exchanges. But four still on death row. Houthi media control
in northwest unabated. Very serious throughout.

2024.assessment:
Very serious press freedom. Houthi Red Sea narrative controlled entirely.
US/UK strikes on Yemen create new restrictions on coverage. Multiple
journalists still imprisoned. Very serious.

1996.assessment:
World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator estimated around 30. Yemen's
statistical system poorly developed. Central Statistical Organisation
operational but limited capacity. Population census rare.
Poor statistical capacity.

1997.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Yemen's CSO producing limited data.
GDP estimates unreliable. Labor statistics minimal.

1998.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Oil price crash reduces government funding
for statistical activities. Data quality and timeliness declining.

1999.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Basic national accounts and some social
statistics produced with international assistance. Poor capacity overall.

2000.assessment:
Poor to moderate statistical capacity. Higher oil revenues allow some
investment in statistical infrastructure. Still poor overall.

2001.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Yemen producing basic macroeconomic data
with external support. Many social indicators missing or unreliable.

2002.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Some improvement with international support.
Still poor overall. Population data from 1994 census becoming outdated.

2003.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Some improvements with IMF/World Bank capacity
building. Basic macroeconomic data improving but social statistics weak.

2004.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. First Houthi war disrupts data collection
in Saada. Conflict areas not covered in surveys.

2005.assessment:
Moderate-low statistical capacity. Some improvement reaching 40.
Multiple conflict areas limiting coverage. Poor to moderate.

2006.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity at border of poor/moderate range.
Third Houthi war limits northern coverage. GDP estimates improving.

2007.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. Some improvements in methodology.
Security challenges limiting field surveys. Border of moderate.

2008.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. Security challenges grow. Fifth Houthi
war and US Embassy bombing limit some data collection. Moderate range.

2009.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity declining. Sixth Houthi war and Saudi
intervention preventing data collection in north. AQAP in south limits
surveys. Declining capacity.

2010.assessment:
Moderate-low statistical capacity. Security challenges in multiple areas
limiting data collection. Border of poor/moderate. Statistical capacity
declining.

2011.assessment:
Statistical capacity declining sharply. Arab Spring and political crisis
destroy statistical infrastructure. CSO funding cut. Field surveys
impossible in conflict areas. Poor statistical capacity.

2012.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Transitional government attempting to restore
statistical functions with international support. Still poor.

2013.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Multiple conflict zones limiting data
collection. Census not conducted. International support helping.
Poor range.

2014.assessment:
Poor statistical capacity. Houthis seize Sanaa disrupting central
statistical organs. Two parallel data collection systems beginning.
Poor and declining.

2015.assessment:
Near-collapse of statistical system. Civil war destroys statistical
infrastructure. CSO split or disbanded. No national data collection
possible. Very poor to no_statistical_system.

2016.assessment:
Statistical system collapsed. No national statistics. Dual authorities
with separate (minimal) data collection. Nearly no_statistical_system.

2017.assessment:
No statistical system. Civil war. Data produced by UN agencies and
NGOs rather than government. Yemen national statistics non-existent.

sources.citation: World Bank (Yemen excluded from WB SCI after 2016)

2018.assessment:
No statistical system. Data produced by humanitarian agencies only.
No national statistical office functioning. No_statistical_system.

2019.assessment:
No statistical system. All economic data from UN agencies, IMF estimates.
National statistical capacity completely absent.

2020.assessment:
No statistical system. COVID-19 exacerbates data vacuum. No census,
no surveys. All data from external estimates.

2021.assessment:
No statistical system. Continuing conflict prevents any systematic
data collection. No_statistical_system.

2022.assessment:
No statistical system. UN truce does not restore statistical capacity.
PLC government has no functioning CSO. No_statistical_system.

2023.assessment:
No statistical system. Ceasefire period does not restore data infrastructure.
No_statistical_system.

2024.assessment:
No statistical system. Ongoing conflict. All data from UN/IMF estimates.
No_statistical_system.
